Meeting ID: ff-20260205-ntw-td-9931

Attendees
- Markus Klein (Redwood Inference)
- Omar El-Sayed (Redwood Inference)
- Yuki Sato (Redwood Inference)
- Ethan Park (Redwood Inference)
- Priya Nair (Northwind Analytics)
- Danielle Chen (Northwind Analytics)
- Alex Romero (Northwind Analytics)
- Tom Bowers (Northwind Analytics)

Summary (auto-generated)
Northwind asked for a deeper walkthrough of how Redwood selects GPU kernels at runtime, specifically around long-context summarization workloads and heterogeneous GPU fleets. Redwood explained kernel auto-selection v2 signals (model family/arch tags, sequence-length regime, GPU SKU normalization), fallback hierarchy, determinism guarantees, and rollout safety controls (feature gates, per-model allow/deny lists, rapid rollback, manual pinning). The group aligned on running a small benchmark matrix on A100 and L40S, with realistic prompt/decode distributions and tail latency monitoring.

Topics (auto-generated)
- Kernel selection: what it is, where it runs, and what inputs it uses
- GPU SKU mapping (A100 variants, MIG, cloud instance naming)
- Long-context performance: prefill vs decode regime and tail latency risk
- Safety mechanisms: fallbacks, determinism/replay, gates, rollback
- Benchmark plan + what data Northwind needs to provide

Questions (auto-generated)
- How do you prevent non-deterministic selection decisions across replicas?
- Can we force/pin a kernel for a specific model and GPU in Dedicated?
- What happens if a kernel is missing or unstable on one SKU?
- How do you detect long-context misclassification before it hits p99?

Transcript

[00:00] Markus Klein: Alright, cool. Folks can hear me okay?

[00:03] Priya Nair: Yep, loud and clear.

[00:05] Markus Klein: Great. Thanks everyone for making time. This one is intended to be more technical than our last call. Omar and Yuki are here from runtime, Ethan from control plane. We can go deep on kernel selection, especially long context.

[00:18] Danielle Chen: Perfect. Yeah, we’re basically trying to de-risk tail latency. Our summarization jobs are like, 12k to 30k input tokens, and then maybe 300 to 1200 output tokens. It’s prefill heavy.

[00:30] Omar El-Sayed: Yep, that’s the classic “prefill dominates” shape. And that’s exactly where bad kernel choice shows up as p99 spikes, not necessarily average.

[00:39] Tom Bowers: And just to set context, we’re an enterprise. We have to answer questions like, can you roll back quickly and can we control it per region. We can’t just yolo a new thing.

[00:49] Markus Klein: Totally. That’s why we wanted Ethan on.

[00:52] Alex Romero: Also we’re comparing to… we’ve tested vLLM on our side, and TensorRT-LLM on a few nodes. So we’re trying to understand what Redwood’s doing differently, or what knobs we’ll get.

[01:03] Markus Klein: Makes sense. Omar, do you wanna start with the “what is kernel selection” for us, like the two-minute overview?

[01:10] Omar El-Sayed: Sure. So, in our runtime we have multiple kernel implementations for key parts of attention and related ops. Depending on model architecture and workload regime, different kernels win. Kernel auto-selection is the module that picks, for a given request, which kernel variant to run.

[01:27] Omar El-Sayed: In v2 specifically, we key selection on three buckets of signals: one, model family/architecture tags (like Llama-style vs Mistral-style, Qwen variants, Gemma-ish), two, sequence-length regime which we treat separately for prefill vs decode behavior, and three, GPU SKU, because H100 vs A100 vs L40S can change the winner.

[01:51] Priya Nair: When you say “request”, do you literally decide per request? Or per model load, like at startup?

[01:57] Yuki Sato: Good question. We decide at request time, but in practice we cache decisions. The selector outputs a decision based on a normalized feature vector. Most of those are stable for many requests: model id + gpu sku + seq bucket. So it’s more like “per regime,” not every time doing heavy computation.

[02:18] Danielle Chen: And your v1 did what?

[02:21] Yuki Sato: v1 was simpler, basically a mostly static mapping keyed on model class and a couple hard-coded thresholds. It didn’t account for SKU differences well, and it treated sequence length too coarsely. It worked for the middle of the distribution but we saw edge cases on long context.

[02:39] Omar El-Sayed: And also v1 had weaker observability. So when you got a latency spike, you couldn’t easily answer “which kernel did we pick” without attaching a profiler.

[02:49] Alex Romero: So v2 is rules? Or some ML thing?

[02:53] Yuki Sato: Rules. We debated a learned scorer, but for safety and explainability and rollback, we landed on rule-based with explicit fallbacks. It’s still a “scoring” concept internally, but it’s deterministic rules and precedence, not a trained model.

[03:10] Priya Nair: Deterministic… like, identical inputs always produce identical output kernel choice?

[03:15] Yuki Sato: Yes. Given the same model metadata, same GPU normalized SKU, same seq bucket inputs, you get the same selection. We’ve added replay tests to ensure that. We also version the rules so if we change thresholds, it’s tracked.

[03:30] Tom Bowers: What about multi-tenant? Like if another customer is running something weird, does it influence our selection?

[03:35] Omar El-Sayed: No. There’s no global learning. The only “dynamic” input can be scheduler hints about observed seq distribution, but those are local to the pool and still deterministic given the hint input. And you can disable that if you want.

[03:49] Ethan Park: Also from the control plane side, for Dedicated you can lock policy. You can say “always use v1 selector” or “use v2 only for these models.” There’s no cross-account mixing.

[04:01] Danielle Chen: Let’s go into the seq bucket thing. Because our problem is long context. How do you bucket?

[04:07] Yuki Sato: We extract prompt length (prefill tokens) and also we estimate expected decode length using either the max_tokens setting or observed from recent traffic. Then we classify into regimes.

[04:19] Yuki Sato: Simplifying a bit: short, medium, long context for prefill; and small vs large decode. For prefill, long context triggers kernels optimized for memory bandwidth and larger tile sizes. The key bug we saw in early testing was misclassifying “very long prompt, small decode” as “mixed,” which picked a kernel optimized for decode throughput but had worse prefill tail.

[04:42] Omar El-Sayed: And that’s exactly the p99 symptom: average ok, but at the long tail you get a 1.5x or 2x spike when you hit 20k+ tokens.

[04:52] Priya Nair: Are you actually measuring prefill vs decode times separately?

[04:56] Omar El-Sayed: Yes, internally we expose “prefill_ms” and “decode_ms” along with chosen kernel id. For canary we look at p95/p99 for each phase. Sometimes total looks fine but prefill is regressing and decode improves, which is not what you want for summarization.

[05:14] Alex Romero: In vLLM we’ve had issues where on L40S certain fused attention kernels are unstable. Do you have that kind of SKU weirdness?

[05:23] Yuki Sato: Yes, and that’s why GPU SKU is a first-class dimension. Some kernels are simply not available on certain CUDA/driver combos or have correctness issues under specific SM versions. So the selector has an explicit fallback hierarchy and a denylist.

[05:38] Danielle Chen: How do you normalize GPU SKU? Because in AWS it’s “p4d” or “p5”, and on our on-prem it’s like “A100-PCIe-80GB”. And then MIG.

[05:49] Ethan Park: Yeah, that comes up constantly. We have a normalization layer that maps cloud instance metadata and device properties into a canonical SKU string. For example, “A100-80GB-SXM4” vs “A100-80GB-PCIe”. We also tag MIG mode.

[06:05] Ethan Park: MIG is important because some kernels assume full GPU resources. So MIG gets treated as a separate SKU class for selection and for allow/deny.

[06:15] Priya Nair: We do use MIG sometimes for batch embeddings. Probably not for summarization, but we might. If MIG is on, do you just force a safe kernel?

[06:23] Yuki Sato: Typically yes. We’ll pick from a smaller candidate set that we’ve validated. And if you pin kernels for a pool, that overrides selection.

[06:32] Tom Bowers: Wait, you keep saying “pin.” Is that like a config knob or do we need support to do it?

[06:37] Ethan Park: It’s a config knob. For Hosted you’d do it via policy rules only in limited cases, but for Dedicated you can set an override: per model id, per GPU class, per region. And it’s hot reload, so you don’t need to restart the runtime.

[06:54] Omar El-Sayed: And there’s also a runtime CLI flag for debugging in non-prod, like “force_kernel=X” and “dump_selection_trace”. You wouldn’t use that in normal ops, but it helps reproduce.

[07:07] Danielle Chen: Ok. What do you log? Because from a security angle, we don’t want prompts in logs.

[07:13] Omar El-Sayed: We do not log prompt content. Telemetry includes model id, normalized sku, seq bucket (like “prefill_long”), selected kernel id, candidate set, and reason codes. No raw tokens.

[07:29] Tom Bowers: Candidate set sounds like it could be high cardinality.

[07:33] Omar El-Sayed: Yes, good call. We sample it. By default, detailed selection events are sampled at a low rate, and aggregated metrics are always on. Also we cap the number of candidates we emit.

[07:45] Priya Nair: So if we’re debugging a p99 spike, can we turn up sampling temporarily?

[07:50] Omar El-Sayed: Yes, with guardrails. You can increase sampling for a specific model or pool, time-bounded.

[08:00] Markus Klein: Just to keep us moving, maybe we switch to the long-context specifics. Danielle, can you share your exact concerns?

[08:08] Danielle Chen: Sure. We’ve seen in our own testing that kernels that are great at decode can be horrible at prefill once you cross like 16k. Our customers will notice if summarization goes from 4s to 8s for a subset. So we want confidence that your selector won’t “flip” at the wrong time.

[08:27] Yuki Sato: Yep. Our approach is: first, conservative thresholds for entering long-context regime. Second, explicit “long-context guardrail” where if prompt_len exceeds a hard cutoff we only consider kernels validated for long prefill. Third, fallback to a known-safe kernel if we detect instability.

[08:46] Alex Romero: Detect instability meaning errors? Or perf instability?

[08:50] Yuki Sato: Both. Errors like illegal memory access, but also perf anomalies like sudden latency increase beyond a threshold. The latter is more delicate because you don’t want flapping. So we mostly rely on rollout monitoring and allow/deny controls rather than automatic flapping.

[09:09] Omar El-Sayed: In practice, we’ll canary v2 for a small percentage and look at deltas in p95/p99 for prefill and total. If we see a spike, we disable v2 for that model+sku combination. That’s the “denylist” path.

[09:24] Priya Nair: How fast can you disable? Like minutes?

[09:27] Ethan Park: Minutes. The policy engine updates propagate typically under 2 minutes to all nodes in a region for Dedicated. Hosted is similar but depends on the layer you’re applying at.

[09:38] Tom Bowers: Who has authority? Us or Redwood?

[09:41] Ethan Park: For Dedicated, you do, via your admin role. Redwood can assist, but you can push the change.

[09:50] Markus Klein: Also for the POC we can set it so your team has that switch and we have a runbook.

[09:56] Danielle Chen: Great. Let’s talk GPU fleets. We currently have A100 80GB on-prem. In cloud we might use L40S for cost. We may evaluate H100 later. Does your selector handle all of those?

[10:10] Yuki Sato: Yes. A100, H100, L40S are in our validated set. L40S has some special cases; we’ve seen certain driver versions where a kernel is fine on one point release and not on another, so we have guardrails that disable v2 on known-bad driver/CUDA combos.

[10:29] Alex Romero: That’s interesting. We had that with TensorRT also. How do you know the driver version?

[10:34] Yuki Sato: We read it from the runtime environment, normalize it, and we match it against a small list. If it’s in the “known unstable” set, we either pick a safe kernel or disable v2 selection entirely.

[10:48] Priya Nair: Is that list public?

[10:50] Markus Klein: We can share what applies to your SKU. It changes, so we typically include it in release notes and a known-issues page.

[10:58] Tom Bowers: From compliance, we need to document any behavior that changes automatically. That’s why I ask.

[11:04] Ethan Park: The guardrail behavior is deterministic and versioned with runtime. It’s not like a remote kill switch that changes without you knowing. Policy changes are auditable.

[11:16] Danielle Chen: Good. Now, about benchmarking. What do you need from us? We can’t give you real prompts.

[11:22] Omar El-Sayed: You don’t need to. What we need is distributions: approximate input token histogram, output token histogram, concurrency levels, and any constraints like “must fit in 80GB.” If you can share a sanitized trace that includes just lengths, that’s perfect.

[11:41] Omar El-Sayed: For the benchmark matrix we want: model family you’ll use, like Llama 70B or 13B, seq buckets that match your workload (12k/16k/24k/32k), and GPUs: A100 and L40S at least. Then we run v1 vs v2 and compare p95/p99.

[11:59] Alex Romero: Are you using continuous batching? Because that changes the picture.

[12:03] Omar El-Sayed: Yes, continuous batching is on. And we also can pass scheduler hints into the selector about observed workload mix. But for the first run we can keep it simple and disable hints so we isolate kernel selection.

[12:18] Priya Nair: Wait, scheduler hints? Like it tells selector “this pool is mostly long context”?

[12:23] Omar El-Sayed: Exactly. It’s basically a rolling window of observed prompt lengths and decode lengths. The selector can choose a kernel that is more stable for that mix.

[12:35] Danielle Chen: But does that make it non-deterministic over time?

[12:39] Yuki Sato: The selection is deterministic given the hint input, but the hint input changes with traffic. So if you want strict replayability, you can either log the hint state or disable hint-based selection. For enterprise we often recommend disabling hints initially.

[12:57] Tom Bowers: For us, simpler is better.

[13:00] Markus Klein: Yeah, we can start with hints off, v2 gated, and then if we want to squeeze perf, we can add hints.

[13:08] Alex Romero: Can you talk about overhead? Like, does evaluating selector add latency? We’re very sensitive.

[13:14] Yuki Sato: It’s tiny. The selector is on the order of microseconds. The bigger risk is telemetry overhead if you emit too much. That’s why we sample. We also cache decisions per (model, sku, seq bucket) so repeated requests don’t even re-evaluate the full rule path.

[13:32] Omar El-Sayed: We have microbench data showing selector+telemetry stays under a small fraction of a millisecond at default sampling.

[13:42] Danielle Chen: Ok. Let’s talk failover. If the selected kernel is wrong, do you auto fallback mid-request?

[13:49] Yuki Sato: Not mid-request, no. That would be complex and potentially non-deterministic. The fallback hierarchy is applied at selection time. If a kernel fails at runtime (like an error), we fail the request and the system can retry with a safe kernel depending on policy. But we try to avoid that by denylists.

[14:09] Priya Nair: So if we see errors, we disable v2 quickly.

[14:12] Ethan Park: Exactly. And the rollback path is: disable v2 for model X on sku Y in region Z, or globally. That reverts to v1 mapping.

[14:25] Tom Bowers: And audit log shows who changed it.

[14:27] Ethan Park: Yep.

[14:30] Markus Klein: Maybe it’s helpful if we describe what “v2 rollout” looks like in Dedicated. Ethan?

[14:36] Ethan Park: Sure. You’d have a feature gate, off by default. Then you can enable for a single model version, in one region, for a small percentage of traffic. You can also do allowlist/denylist. If metrics regress beyond your thresholds, you flip it off. Changes are quick and reversible.

[14:56] Danielle Chen: When you say percentage, how do you route? Sticky per request? per pod?

[15:01] Ethan Park: Typically per pod or per shard of the pool, so it’s stable. We avoid per-request randomization for determinism and to reduce noise.

[15:12] Alex Romero: Great. Now, we also care about cost. L40S is cheaper. But long context on L40S might be slower. Does v2 help there?

[15:20] Omar El-Sayed: It can. L40S tends to have different sweet spots. With v2, we pick kernels tuned for that SKU which can reduce variance. It won’t magically beat H100, but it reduces p99 spikes and makes throughput more predictable.

[15:36] Priya Nair: How do you measure “predictable”?

[15:39] Omar El-Sayed: Standard deviation of per-request latency, plus p95/p99 gap. We also look at queueing time under load. Mis-selection can cause queueing cascades.

[15:52] Danielle Chen: That’s the big one. Cascades.

[15:55] Omar El-Sayed: Yep.

[15:58] (crosstalk)

[16:00] Markus Klein: Sorry, go ahead Danielle.

[16:02] Danielle Chen: No, just +1. We’ve seen that in Triton too, where one slow request blocks the batch.

[16:07] Omar El-Sayed: Exactly. So for your summarization workload, we’ll focus on the long-context prefill regime.

[16:15] Alex Romero: What models do you assume we’ll use? We’re currently evaluating Llama 3-ish 70B, and also a smaller 8B for some tasks.

[16:24] Markus Klein: Redwood supports those families. Yuki, anything on model-family tagging?

[16:30] Yuki Sato: Yeah. We tag by architecture based on model metadata/config. Sometimes people fine-tune and rename and the tag is missing. In v2 we have a classifier that inspects config fields to determine family. If it can’t, we fall back to a conservative default kernel set.

[16:50] Priya Nair: So “unknown model family” doesn’t break.

[16:53] Yuki Sato: Correct. You just get safe behavior.

[16:57] Tom Bowers: Safe means slower?

[16:59] Yuki Sato: Potentially slightly slower, yes, but stable.

[17:05] Danielle Chen: That’s fine for first launch.

[17:08] Markus Klein: Cool. Tom, anything else on security while we’re here?

[17:12] Tom Bowers: Just data retention and logging. You said no prompts in telemetry. For Dedicated, can logs stay in our account?

[17:19] Ethan Park: Yes. Dedicated can forward logs/metrics to your sink. We can also provide audit logs and integrate with your KMS in some setups. For this benchmark we’ll keep it minimal.

[17:32] Tom Bowers: Ok.

[17:34] Priya Nair: Back to the benchmark: what does “success” look like? Like, what deltas would you expect?

[17:40] Omar El-Sayed: It depends, but we generally aim for: no correctness regressions, and improved p95/p99 or at least reduced variance. For long-context, even a 10-15% improvement in p99 prefill is meaningful. Sometimes v2 is neutral but more stable across SKUs.

[17:58] Danielle Chen: Neutral average but improved tail is still a win.

[18:02] Omar El-Sayed: Exactly.

[18:05] Alex Romero: Do you have any customer story on long context?

[18:09] Markus Klein: We can share anonymized examples. But for now, the best is we run your workload distribution.

[18:17] Priya Nair: Agreed.

[18:20] Yuki Sato: Also, we’ll include a stress scenario: mixed workloads, where some requests are short and some are long. That’s where misclassification and batching interactions show up.

[18:34] Danielle Chen: Yep, because we’re not purely summarization. There’s chat-like stuff too.

[18:39] Omar El-Sayed: Great. We’ll include mixed.

[18:42] Markus Klein: Let’s do a quick “GPU inventory” check. Priya, you said A100 80s on-prem. In cloud, L40S maybe. Any H100?

[18:52] Priya Nair: Not today. H100 maybe next quarter. Also we have some A100 40GB, but we avoid them for long context.

[18:59] Yuki Sato: A100 40 vs 80 matters, yes. We treat that as separate SKU class.

[19:05] Alex Romero: And do you support splitting KV cache across GPUs? Not sure if relevant.

[19:10] Omar El-Sayed: We do tensor/pipeline parallel depending on model and pool config, but for kernel selection discussion we’re mostly within a GPU’s kernels. Multi-GPU changes throughput but kernels are still SKU-specific.

[19:25] Danielle Chen: Ok.

[19:27] Priya Nair: One more question: in vLLM, we sometimes had “it depends on batch size” for which kernel is best. Are you factoring batch size?

[19:35] Yuki Sato: Indirectly. We use sequence regime and, optionally, scheduler-provided batching hints. We don’t take “batch size” as a primary feature because it varies too quickly and makes selection unstable. But we choose kernels that are robust across a range of batch sizes for that regime.

[19:56] Omar El-Sayed: And if you have a dedicated pool that runs mostly one workload, the scheduler hints can help lock in the stable choice.

[20:07] Tom Bowers: That hint thing again. I’ll want it off in our first tests.

[20:11] Markus Klein: Understood.

[20:13] Alex Romero: Are you using Triton under the hood?

[20:16] Omar El-Sayed: No, we have our own serving runtime. Some kernels are built with CUDA and use Triton-language in places, but it’s not Triton Inference Server as the orchestration layer.

[20:28] Alex Romero: Got it.

[20:30] Danielle Chen: How do we debug if we see an anomaly? Like p99 spike at 24k.

[20:36] Omar El-Sayed: You’d look at dashboards: selection distribution by seq bucket, per-kernel latency histograms, and compare v1 vs v2. If v2 is picking “Kernel A” for long prefill and it correlates with spikes, we can denylist Kernel A for that SKU or adjust threshold. In emergencies you just disable v2.

[20:57] Ethan Park: And you can pin “safe kernel” for that model and pool.

[21:01] Priya Nair: Is there a notion of “known safe kernel” per family?

[21:05] Yuki Sato: Yes. There’s always a baseline kernel that we trust broadly. It may not be the fastest, but it’s stable.

[21:14] Danielle Chen: That’s basically what we want: a stable baseline and then an opt-in faster path.

[21:20] Markus Klein: Perfect. That aligns with how we ship v2.

[21:24] (brief silence)

[21:26] Markus Klein: Let me check time. We have about 35 minutes left. Anything else on the selection logic you want to poke at?

[21:33] Alex Romero: Yeah, how big is the candidate set? Like, do you have 2 kernels or 20?

[21:39] Yuki Sato: Depends on model family. Typically single-digit candidates, maybe 3-6 for attention. Then there’s additional kernels for other ops, but selection focus is attention path. We don’t want an explosion.

[21:56] Alex Romero: And you said you emit “reason codes.” What are those?

[22:01] Yuki Sato: Things like “gpu_sku_match,” “seq_bucket_long_prefill,” “kernel_denied_by_driver_guardrail,” “model_family_unknown_fallback.” It helps explain why you landed on a choice.

[22:16] Priya Nair: Nice.

[22:18] Tom Bowers: From an audit perspective, that’s also helpful. If we ever have to justify “why did we change behavior.”

[22:24] Ethan Park: Exactly.

[22:26] Danielle Chen: Ok, can we talk about how you’ll run the benchmark? Are we doing it ourselves or Redwood runs it?

[22:33] Markus Klein: Either. For quickest, we can run it in a Redwood Dedicated test pool and share results. If you need to run in your environment, we can provide the harness.

[22:46] Priya Nair: We probably want both. Because we need to trust it translates to our on-prem.

[22:52] Omar El-Sayed: For on-prem translation, the two biggest variables are GPU SKU and driver version. If those match, results correlate well. If they don’t, we’ll note differences.

[23:05] Danielle Chen: On-prem driver is usually behind, honestly.

[23:08] Yuki Sato: Then we should pay extra attention to the driver guardrails. If you’re on a known-problem version, we might recommend a safe kernel or staying on v1 until updated.

[23:22] Alex Romero: That’s fair.

[23:24] Markus Klein: Another question I’ve heard: “does v2 change outputs?” It shouldn’t. But maybe Omar can say something.

[23:31] Omar El-Sayed: Outputs should be identical. Kernels implement the same math within expected FP tolerances. We have correctness tests. If you enable deterministic modes, we keep nondeterminism minimized. But I’ll be candid: some kernels have different numerical behavior at the very low bits, which should not affect generation in practice. We gate anything that shows meaningful divergence.

[23:55] Priya Nair: We do have eval harness. We can check.

[23:58] Omar El-Sayed: Great. We can align on what evals matter.

[24:02] Danielle Chen: Ok. How do we coordinate? Should we send you distributions today?

[24:07] Markus Klein: Yeah, if you can send token length histograms and concurrency targets, we can prep the matrix.

[24:14] Ethan Park: Also confirm whether you need VPC peering for the Dedicated pool. It changes provisioning time.

[24:21] Priya Nair: For the benchmark, we can probably do it without VPC if we’re not sending data. But for later production, yes.

[24:29] Markus Klein: Great.

[24:31] Tom Bowers: One more: data residency. For now, US is fine.

[24:35] Markus Klein: Cool.

[24:38] Alex Romero: Since we mentioned competitors—Together, SageMaker endpoints—one thing they struggle with is “explain why performance changed.” Your telemetry seems to help.

[24:48] Omar El-Sayed: That’s the goal. The “selection trace” is basically “why.”

[24:53] Danielle Chen: Are dashboards included or extra?

[24:57] Markus Klein: In Dedicated, you get baseline dashboards. For deeper retention it can be an add-on, but for POC we’ll make sure you have what you need.

[25:06] Priya Nair: Ok.

[25:08] (crosstalk)

[25:09] Danielle Chen: Sorry, one more technical: does selection happen before compilation or after? Like, do you compile per kernel?

[25:17] Yuki Sato: Selection happens at runtime before execution, but after we know the model config. Some kernels have compiled variants. We keep a compilation cache keyed on model+kernel+sku and reuse.

[25:32] Alex Romero: So if kernel changes, you might hit compile overhead?

[25:36] Yuki Sato: Potentially on first use, yes. That’s another reason we avoid flapping and we cache decisions.

[25:44] Omar El-Sayed: And in canary we warm it up before measuring.

[25:49] Danielle Chen: Great.

[25:52] Priya Nair: I think we’ve got what we need. Can we recap deliverables?

[25:57] Markus Klein: Sure, let’s do a recap now and then we can use remaining time for any last Qs.

[26:03] Markus Klein: Redwood side: we’ll send benchmark harness instructions, plus recommended prompt/decode distributions for long-context summarization. We’ll provision a Dedicated test pool in us-east with A100-80GB and L40S. We’ll set v2 behind a gate, hints off, and run v1 vs v2 comparisons focusing on prefill p95/p99.

[26:27] Markus Klein: Northwind side: you’ll confirm target GPUs and whether MIG is enabled. You’ll share sanitized length distributions and concurrency targets. And then we schedule a follow-up to review results.

[26:40] Priya Nair: Sounds right.

[26:42] Tom Bowers: And you’ll also share how rollback works, like the admin control and audit.

[26:47] Ethan Park: Yep, I’ll include that in a note.

[26:51] Omar El-Sayed: I’ll send the telemetry field list and recommended sampling for canary.

[26:56] Yuki Sato: And I’ll send the driver guardrail summary for the SKUs you care about.

[27:01] Danielle Chen: Perfect.

[27:04] Alex Romero: While we have you, can I ask something about L40S? We saw weirdness around attention “flash” kernel. Do you use flash attention?

[27:13] Yuki Sato: We have flash-style kernels, yes. But exact implementation differs by SKU and driver. Some combinations are on denylist. So for L40S we might pick a different kernel than on A100.

[27:28] Alex Romero: Cool. That’s what we wanted.

[27:31] Priya Nair: Ok, last question for me: if we later go Private deployment, does the same selector exist?

[27:38] Markus Klein: Yes. Same runtime, same selection logic. Control plane differs but the knobs exist. For air-gapped, we ship the policy config with the package.

[27:50] Tom Bowers: Good.

[27:52] Markus Klein: Anything else?

[27:54] Danielle Chen: Not from me.

[27:56] Alex Romero: No, good.

[27:58] Priya Nair: All good.

[28:00] Markus Klein: Great. We’ll follow up tomorrow with the recap and proposed benchmark schedule. Thanks all.

[28:07] (meeting continues with light scheduling chatter; transcript truncated by Fireflies)

Notes
- During the call, Redwood referenced internal dashboard panels: selection distribution by model/GPU/seq bucket, prefill/decode latency breakdowns, and rollback trigger thresholds.
- Northwind indicated primary workload: long-context summarization (12k-30k input tokens) with moderate decode (300-1200 output tokens) and sensitivity to p99.
- Benchmark to prioritize A100-80GB and L40S; MIG considerations to be confirmed.
text:
Fireflies.ai - Meeting Transcript

Title: Technical deep dive - runtime performance + kernel selection (Redwood x Northwind)
Date: 2026-02-05
Time: 10:00 AM - 10:56 AM PT
Duration: 56 minutes
Meeting ID: ff-20260205-ntw-td-9931

Attendees
- Markus Klein (Redwood Inference)
- Omar El-Sayed (Redwood Inference)
- Yuki Sato (Redwood Inference)
- Ethan Park (Redwood Inference)
- Priya Nair (Northwind Analytics)
- Danielle Chen (Northwind Analytics)
- Alex Romero (Northwind Analytics)
- Tom Bowers (Northwind Analytics)

Summary (auto-generated)
Northwind asked for a deeper walkthrough of how Redwood selects GPU kernels at runtime, specifically around long-context summarization workloads and heterogeneous GPU fleets. Redwood explained kernel auto-selection v2 signals (model family/arch tags, sequence-length regime, GPU SKU normalization), fallback hierarchy, determinism guarantees, and rollout safety controls (feature gates, per-model allow/deny lists, rapid rollback, manual pinning). The group aligned on running a small benchmark matrix on A100 and L40S, with realistic prompt/decode distributions and tail latency monitoring.

Topics (auto-generated)
- Kernel selection: what it is, where it runs, and what inputs it uses
- GPU SKU mapping (A100 variants, MIG, cloud instance naming)
- Long-context performance: prefill vs decode regime and tail latency risk
- Safety mechanisms: fallbacks, determinism/replay, gates, rollback
- Benchmark plan + what data Northwind needs to provide

Questions (auto-generated)
- How do you prevent non-deterministic selection decisions across replicas?
- Can we force/pin a kernel for a specific model and GPU in Dedicated?
- What happens if a kernel is missing or unstable on one SKU?
- How do you detect long-context misclassification before it hits p99?

Transcript

[00:00] Markus Klein: Alright, cool. Folks can hear me okay?

[00:03] Priya Nair: Yep, loud and clear.

[00:05] Markus Klein: Great. Thanks everyone for making time. This one is intended to be more technical than our last call. Omar and Yuki are here from runtime, Ethan from control plane. We can go deep on kernel selection, especially long context.

[00:18] Danielle Chen: Perfect. Yeah, we’re basically trying to de-risk tail latency. Our summarization jobs are like, 12k to 30k input tokens, and then maybe 300 to 1200 output tokens. It’s prefill heavy.

[00:30] Omar El-Sayed: Yep, that’s the classic “prefill dominates” shape. And that’s exactly where bad kernel choice shows up as p99 spikes, not necessarily average.

[00:39] Tom Bowers: And just to set context, we’re an enterprise. We have to answer questions like, can you roll back quickly and can we control it per region. We can’t just yolo a new thing.

[00:49] Markus Klein: Totally. That’s why we wanted Ethan on.

[00:52] Alex Romero: Also we’re comparing to… we’ve tested vLLM on our side, and TensorRT-LLM on a few nodes. So we’re trying to understand what Redwood’s doing differently, or what knobs we’ll get.

[01:03] Markus Klein: Makes sense. Omar, do you wanna start with the “what is kernel selection” for us, like the two-minute overview?

[01:10] Omar El-Sayed: Sure. So, in our runtime we have multiple kernel implementations for key parts of attention and related ops. Depending on model architecture and workload regime, different kernels win. Kernel auto-selection is the module that picks, for a given request, which kernel variant to run.

[01:27] Omar El-Sayed: In v2 specifically, we key selection on three buckets of signals: one, model family/architecture tags (like Llama-style vs Mistral-style, Qwen variants, Gemma-ish), two, sequence-length regime which we treat separately for prefill vs decode behavior, and three, GPU SKU, because H100 vs A100 vs L40S can change the winner.

[01:51] Priya Nair: When you say “request”, do you literally decide per request? Or per model load, like at startup?

[01:57] Yuki Sato: Good question. We decide at request time, but in practice we cache decisions. The selector outputs a decision based on a normalized feature vector. Most of those are stable for many requests: model id + gpu sku + seq bucket. So it’s more like “per regime,” not every time doing heavy computation.

[02:18] Danielle Chen: And your v1 did what?

[02:21] Yuki Sato: v1 was simpler, basically a mostly static mapping keyed on model class and a couple hard-coded thresholds. It didn’t account for SKU differences well, and it treated sequence length too coarsely. It worked for the middle of the distribution but we saw edge cases on long context.

[02:39] Omar El-Sayed: And also v1 had weaker observability. So when you got a latency spike, you couldn’t easily answer “which kernel did we pick” without attaching a profiler.

[02:49] Alex Romero: So v2 is rules? Or some ML thing?

[02:53] Yuki Sato: Rules. We debated a learned scorer, but for safety and explainability and rollback, we landed on rule-based with explicit fallbacks. It’s still a “scoring” concept internally, but it’s deterministic rules and precedence, not a trained model.

[03:10] Priya Nair: Deterministic… like, identical inputs always produce identical output kernel choice?

[03:15] Yuki Sato: Yes. Given the same model metadata, same GPU normalized SKU, same seq bucket inputs, you get the same selection. We’ve added replay tests to ensure that. We also version the rules so if we change thresholds, it’s tracked.

[03:30] Tom Bowers: What about multi-tenant? Like if another customer is running something weird, does it influence our selection?

[03:35] Omar El-Sayed: No. There’s no global learning. The only “dynamic” input can be scheduler hints about observed seq distribution, but those are local to the pool and still deterministic given the hint input. And you can disable that if you want.

[03:49] Ethan Park: Also from the control plane side, for Dedicated you can lock policy. You can say “always use v1 selector” or “use v2 only for these models.” There’s no cross-account mixing.

[04:01] Danielle Chen: Let’s go into the seq bucket thing. Because our problem is long context. How do you bucket?

[04:07] Yuki Sato: We extract prompt length (prefill tokens) and also we estimate expected decode length using either the max_tokens setting or observed from recent traffic. Then we classify into regimes.

[04:19] Yuki Sato: Simplifying a bit: short, medium, long context for prefill; and small vs large decode. For prefill, long context triggers kernels optimized for memory bandwidth and larger tile sizes. The key bug we saw in early testing was misclassifying “very long prompt, small decode” as “mixed,” which picked a kernel optimized for decode throughput but had worse prefill tail.

[04:42] Omar El-Sayed: And that’s exactly the p99 symptom: average ok, but at the long tail you get a 1.5x or 2x spike when you hit 20k+ tokens.

[04:52] Priya Nair: Are you actually measuring prefill vs decode times separately?

[04:56] Omar El-Sayed: Yes, internally we expose “prefill_ms” and “decode_ms” along with chosen kernel id. For canary we look at p95/p99 for each phase. Sometimes total looks fine but prefill is regressing and decode improves, which is not what you want for summarization.

[05:14] Alex Romero: In vLLM we’ve had issues where on L40S certain fused attention kernels are unstable. Do you have that kind of SKU weirdness?

[05:23] Yuki Sato: Yes, and that’s why GPU SKU is a first-class dimension. Some kernels are simply not available on certain CUDA/driver combos or have correctness issues under specific SM versions. So the selector has an explicit fallback hierarchy and a denylist.

[05:38] Danielle Chen: How do you normalize GPU SKU? Because in AWS it’s “p4d” or “p5”, and on our on-prem it’s like “A100-PCIe-80GB”. And then MIG.

[05:49] Ethan Park: Yeah, that comes up constantly. We have a normalization layer that maps cloud instance metadata and device properties into a canonical SKU string. For example, “A100-80GB-SXM4” vs “A100-80GB-PCIe”. We also tag MIG mode.

[06:05] Ethan Park: MIG is important because some kernels assume full GPU resources. So MIG gets treated as a separate SKU class for selection and for allow/deny.

[06:15] Priya Nair: We do use MIG sometimes for batch embeddings. Probably not for summarization, but we might. If MIG is on, do you just force a safe kernel?

[06:23] Yuki Sato: Typically yes. We’ll pick from a smaller candidate set that we’ve validated. And if you pin kernels for a pool, that overrides selection.

[06:32] Tom Bowers: Wait, you keep saying “pin.” Is that like a config knob or do we need support to do it?

[06:37] Ethan Park: It’s a config knob. For Hosted you’d do it via policy rules only in limited cases, but for Dedicated you can set an override: per model id, per GPU class, per region. And it’s hot reload, so you don’t need to restart the runtime.

[06:54] Omar El-Sayed: And there’s also a runtime CLI flag for debugging in non-prod, like “force_kernel=X” and “dump_selection_trace”. You wouldn’t use that in normal ops, but it helps reproduce.

[07:07] Danielle Chen: Ok. What do you log? Because from a security angle, we don’t want prompts in logs.

[07:13] Omar El-Sayed: We do not log prompt content. Telemetry includes model id, normalized sku, seq bucket (like “prefill_long”), selected kernel id, candidate set, and reason codes. No raw tokens.

[07:29] Tom Bowers: Candidate set sounds like it could be high cardinality.

[07:33] Omar El-Sayed: Yes, good call. We sample it. By default, detailed selection events are sampled at a low rate, and aggregated metrics are always on. Also we cap the number of candidates we emit.

[07:45] Priya Nair: So if we’re debugging a p99 spike, can we turn up sampling temporarily?

[07:50] Omar El-Sayed: Yes, with guardrails. You can increase sampling for a specific model or pool, time-bounded.

[08:00] Markus Klein: Just to keep us moving, maybe we switch to the long-context specifics. Danielle, can you share your exact concerns?

[08:08] Danielle Chen: Sure. We’ve seen in our own testing that kernels that are great at decode can be horrible at prefill once you cross like 16k. Our customers will notice if summarization goes from 4s to 8s for a subset. So we want confidence that your selector won’t “flip” at the wrong time.

[08:27] Yuki Sato: Yep. Our approach is: first, conservative thresholds for entering long-context regime. Second, explicit “long-context guardrail” where if prompt_len exceeds a hard cutoff we only consider kernels validated for long prefill. Third, fallback to a known-safe kernel if we detect instability.

[08:46] Alex Romero: Detect instability meaning errors? Or perf instability?

[08:50] Yuki Sato: Both. Errors like illegal memory access, but also perf anomalies like sudden latency increase beyond a threshold. The latter is more delicate because you don’t want flapping. So we mostly rely on rollout monitoring and allow/deny controls rather than automatic flapping.

[09:09] Omar El-Sayed: In practice, we’ll canary v2 for a small percentage and look at deltas in p95/p99 for prefill and total. If we see a spike, we disable v2 for that model+sku combination. That’s the “denylist” path.

[09:24] Priya Nair: How fast can you disable? Like minutes?

[09:27] Ethan Park: Minutes. The policy engine updates propagate typically under 2 minutes to all nodes in a region for Dedicated. Hosted is similar but depends on the layer you’re applying at.

[09:38] Tom Bowers: Who has authority? Us or Redwood?

[09:41] Ethan Park: For Dedicated, you do, via your admin role. Redwood can assist, but you can push the change.

[09:50] Markus Klein: Also for the POC we can set it so your team has that switch and we have a runbook.

[09:56] Danielle Chen: Great. Let’s talk GPU fleets. We currently have A100 80GB on-prem. In cloud we might use L40S for cost. We may evaluate H100 later. Does your selector handle all of those?

[10:10] Yuki Sato: Yes. A100, H100, L40S are in our validated set. L40S has some special cases; we’ve seen certain driver versions where a kernel is fine on one point release and not on another, so we have guardrails that disable v2 on known-bad driver/CUDA combos.

[10:29] Alex Romero: That’s interesting. We had that with TensorRT also. How do you know the driver version?

[10:34] Yuki Sato: We read it from the runtime environment, normalize it, and we match it against a small list. If it’s in the “known unstable” set, we either pick a safe kernel or disable v2 selection entirely.

[10:48] Priya Nair: Is that list public?

[10:50] Markus Klein: We can share what applies to your SKU. It changes, so we typically include it in release notes and a known-issues page.

[10:58] Tom Bowers: From compliance, we need to document any behavior that changes automatically. That’s why I ask.

[11:04] Ethan Park: The guardrail behavior is deterministic and versioned with runtime. It’s not like a remote kill switch that changes without you knowing. Policy changes are auditable.

[11:16] Danielle Chen: Good. Now, about benchmarking. What do you need from us? We can’t give you real prompts.

[11:22] Omar El-Sayed: You don’t need to. What we need is distributions: approximate input token histogram, output token histogram, concurrency levels, and any constraints like “must fit in 80GB.” If you can share a sanitized trace that includes just lengths, that’s perfect.

[11:41] Omar El-Sayed: For the benchmark matrix we want: model family you’ll use, like Llama 70B or 13B, seq buckets that match your workload (12k/16k/24k/32k), and GPUs: A100 and L40S at least. Then we run v1 vs v2 and compare p95/p99.

[11:59] Alex Romero: Are you using continuous batching? Because that changes the picture.

[12:03] Omar El-Sayed: Yes, continuous batching is on. And we also can pass scheduler hints into the selector about observed workload mix. But for the first run we can keep it simple and disable hints so we isolate kernel selection.

[12:18] Priya Nair: Wait, scheduler hints? Like it tells selector “this pool is mostly long context”?

[12:23] Omar El-Sayed: Exactly. It’s basically a rolling window of observed prompt lengths and decode lengths. The selector can choose a kernel that is more stable for that mix.

[12:35] Danielle Chen: But does that make it non-deterministic over time?

[12:39] Yuki Sato: The selection is deterministic given the hint input, but the hint input changes with traffic. So if you want strict replayability, you can either log the hint state or disable hint-based selection. For enterprise we often recommend disabling hints initially.

[12:57] Tom Bowers: For us, simpler is better.

[13:00] Markus Klein: Yeah, we can start with hints off, v2 gated, and then if we want to squeeze perf, we can add hints.

[13:08] Alex Romero: Can you talk about overhead? Like, does evaluating selector add latency? We’re very sensitive.

[13:14] Yuki Sato: It’s tiny. The selector is on the order of microseconds. The bigger risk is telemetry overhead if you emit too much. That’s why we sample. We also cache decisions per (model, sku, seq bucket) so repeated requests don’t even re-evaluate the full rule path.

[13:32] Omar El-Sayed: We have microbench data showing selector+telemetry stays under a small fraction of a millisecond at default sampling.

[13:42] Danielle Chen: Ok. Let’s talk failover. If the selected kernel is wrong, do you auto fallback mid-request?

[13:49] Yuki Sato: Not mid-request, no. That would be complex and potentially non-deterministic. The fallback hierarchy is applied at selection time. If a kernel fails at runtime (like an error), we fail the request and the system can retry with a safe kernel depending on policy. But we try to avoid that by denylists.

[14:09] Priya Nair: So if we see errors, we disable v2 quickly.

[14:12] Ethan Park: Exactly. And the rollback path is: disable v2 for model X on sku Y in region Z, or globally. That reverts to v1 mapping.

[14:25] Tom Bowers: And audit log shows who changed it.

[14:27] Ethan Park: Yep.

[14:30] Markus Klein: Maybe it’s helpful if we describe what “v2 rollout” looks like in Dedicated. Ethan?

[14:36] Ethan Park: Sure. You’d have a feature gate, off by default. Then you can enable for a single model version, in one region, for a small percentage of traffic. You can also do allowlist/denylist. If metrics regress beyond your thresholds, you flip it off. Changes are quick and reversible.

[14:56] Danielle Chen: When you say percentage, how do you route? Sticky per request? per pod?

[15:01] Ethan Park: Typically per pod or per shard of the pool, so it’s stable. We avoid per-request randomization for determinism and to reduce noise.

[15:12] Alex Romero: Great. Now, we also care about cost. L40S is cheaper. But long context on L40S might be slower. Does v2 help there?

[15:20] Omar El-Sayed: It can. L40S tends to have different sweet spots. With v2, we pick kernels tuned for that SKU which can reduce variance. It won’t magically beat H100, but it reduces p99 spikes and makes throughput more predictable.

[15:36] Priya Nair: How do you measure “predictable”?

[15:39] Omar El-Sayed: Standard deviation of per-request latency, plus p95/p99 gap. We also look at queueing time under load. Mis-selection can cause queueing cascades.

[15:52] Danielle Chen: That’s the big one. Cascades.

[15:55] Omar El-Sayed: Yep.

[15:58] (crosstalk)

[16:00] Markus Klein: Sorry, go ahead Danielle.

[16:02] Danielle Chen: No, just +1. We’ve seen that in Triton too, where one slow request blocks the batch.

[16:07] Omar El-Sayed: Exactly. So for your summarization workload, we’ll focus on the long-context prefill regime.

[16:15] Alex Romero: What models do you assume we’ll use? We’re currently evaluating Llama 3-ish 70B, and also a smaller 8B for some tasks.

[16:24] Markus Klein: Redwood supports those families. Yuki, anything on model-family tagging?

[16:30] Yuki Sato: Yeah. We tag by architecture based on model metadata/config. Sometimes people fine-tune and rename and the tag is missing. In v2 we have a classifier that inspects config fields to determine family. If it can’t, we fall back to a conservative default kernel set.

[16:50] Priya Nair: So “unknown model family” doesn’t break.

[16:53] Yuki Sato: Correct. You just get safe behavior.

[16:57] Tom Bowers: Safe means slower?

[16:59] Yuki Sato: Potentially slightly slower, yes, but stable.

[17:05] Danielle Chen: That’s fine for first launch.

[17:08] Markus Klein: Cool. Tom, anything else on security while we’re here?

[17:12] Tom Bowers: Just data retention and logging. You said no prompts in telemetry. For Dedicated, can logs stay in our account?

[17:19] Ethan Park: Yes. Dedicated can forward logs/metrics to your sink. We can also provide audit logs and integrate with your KMS in some setups. For this benchmark we’ll keep it minimal.

[17:32] Tom Bowers: Ok.

[17:34] Priya Nair: Back to the benchmark: what does “success” look like? Like, what deltas would you expect?

[17:40] Omar El-Sayed: It depends, but we generally aim for: no correctness regressions, and improved p95/p99 or at least reduced variance. For long-context, even a 10-15% improvement in p99 prefill is meaningful. Sometimes v2 is neutral but more stable across SKUs.

[17:58] Danielle Chen: Neutral average but improved tail is still a win.

[18:02] Omar El-Sayed: Exactly.

[18:05] Alex Romero: Do you have any customer story on long context?

[18:09] Markus Klein: We can share anonymized examples. But for now, the best is we run your workload distribution.

[18:17] Priya Nair: Agreed.

[18:20] Yuki Sato: Also, we’ll include a stress scenario: mixed workloads, where some requests are short and some are long. That’s where misclassification and batching interactions show up.

[18:34] Danielle Chen: Yep, because we’re not purely summarization. There’s chat-like stuff too.

[18:39] Omar El-Sayed: Great. We’ll include mixed.

[18:42] Markus Klein: Let’s do a quick “GPU inventory” check. Priya, you said A100 80s on-prem. In cloud, L40S maybe. Any H100?

[18:52] Priya Nair: Not today. H100 maybe next quarter. Also we have some A100 40GB, but we avoid them for long context.

[18:59] Yuki Sato: A100 40 vs 80 matters, yes. We treat that as separate SKU class.

[19:05] Alex Romero: And do you support splitting KV cache across GPUs? Not sure if relevant.

[19:10] Omar El-Sayed: We do tensor/pipeline parallel depending on model and pool config, but for kernel selection discussion we’re mostly within a GPU’s kernels. Multi-GPU changes throughput but kernels are still SKU-specific.

[19:25] Danielle Chen: Ok.

[19:27] Priya Nair: One more question: in vLLM, we sometimes had “it depends on batch size” for which kernel is best. Are you factoring batch size?

[19:35] Yuki Sato: Indirectly. We use sequence regime and, optionally, scheduler-provided batching hints. We don’t take “batch size” as a primary feature because it varies too quickly and makes selection unstable. But we choose kernels that are robust across a range of batch sizes for that regime.

[19:56] Omar El-Sayed: And if you have a dedicated pool that runs mostly one workload, the scheduler hints can help lock in the stable choice.

[20:07] Tom Bowers: That hint thing again. I’ll want it off in our first tests.

[20:11] Markus Klein: Understood.

[20:13] Alex Romero: Are you using Triton under the hood?

[20:16] Omar El-Sayed: No, we have our own serving runtime. Some kernels are built with CUDA and use Triton-language in places, but it’s not Triton Inference Server as the orchestration layer.

[20:28] Alex Romero: Got it.

[20:30] Danielle Chen: How do we debug if we see an anomaly? Like p99 spike at 24k.

[20:36] Omar El-Sayed: You’d look at dashboards: selection distribution by seq bucket, per-kernel latency histograms, and compare v1 vs v2. If v2 is picking “Kernel A” for long prefill and it correlates with spikes, we can denylist Kernel A for that SKU or adjust threshold. In emergencies you just disable v2.

[20:57] Ethan Park: And you can pin “safe kernel” for that model and pool.

[21:01] Priya Nair: Is there a notion of “known safe kernel” per family?

[21:05] Yuki Sato: Yes. There’s always a baseline kernel that we trust broadly. It may not be the fastest, but it’s stable.

[21:14] Danielle Chen: That’s basically what we want: a stable baseline and then an opt-in faster path.

[21:20] Markus Klein: Perfect. That aligns with how we ship v2.

[21:24] (brief silence)

[21:26] Markus Klein: Let me check time. We have about 35 minutes left. Anything else on the selection logic you want to poke at?

[21:33] Alex Romero: Yeah, how big is the candidate set? Like, do you have 2 kernels or 20?

[21:39] Yuki Sato: Depends on model family. Typically single-digit candidates, maybe 3-6 for attention. Then there’s additional kernels for other ops, but selection focus is attention path. We don’t want an explosion.

[21:56] Alex Romero: And you said you emit “reason codes.” What are those?

[22:01] Yuki Sato: Things like “gpu_sku_match,” “seq_bucket_long_prefill,” “kernel_denied_by_driver_guardrail,” “model_family_unknown_fallback.” It helps explain why you landed on a choice.

[22:16] Priya Nair: Nice.

[22:18] Tom Bowers: From an audit perspective, that’s also helpful. If we ever have to justify “why did we change behavior.”

[22:24] Ethan Park: Exactly.

[22:26] Danielle Chen: Ok, can we talk about how you’ll run the benchmark? Are we doing it ourselves or Redwood runs it?

[22:33] Markus Klein: Either. For quickest, we can run it in a Redwood Dedicated test pool and share results. If you need to run in your environment, we can provide the harness.

[22:46] Priya Nair: We probably want both. Because we need to trust it translates to our on-prem.

[22:52] Omar El-Sayed: For on-prem translation, the two biggest variables are GPU SKU and driver version. If those match, results correlate well. If they don’t, we’ll note differences.

[23:05] Danielle Chen: On-prem driver is usually behind, honestly.

[23:08] Yuki Sato: Then we should pay extra attention to the driver guardrails. If you’re on a known-problem version, we might recommend a safe kernel or staying on v1 until updated.

[23:22] Alex Romero: That’s fair.

[23:24] Markus Klein: Another question I’ve heard: “does v2 change outputs?” It shouldn’t. But maybe Omar can say something.

[23:31] Omar El-Sayed: Outputs should be identical. Kernels implement the same math within expected FP tolerances. We have correctness tests. If you enable deterministic modes, we keep nondeterminism minimized. But I’ll be candid: some kernels have different numerical behavior at the very low bits, which should not affect generation in practice. We gate anything that shows meaningful divergence.

[23:55] Priya Nair: We do have eval harness. We can check.

[23:58] Omar El-Sayed: Great. We can align on what evals matter.

[24:02] Danielle Chen: Ok. How do we coordinate? Should we send you distributions today?

[24:07] Markus Klein: Yeah, if you can send token length histograms and concurrency targets, we can prep the matrix.

[24:14] Ethan Park: Also confirm whether you need VPC peering for the Dedicated pool. It changes provisioning time.

[24:21] Priya Nair: For the benchmark, we can probably do it without VPC if we’re not sending data. But for later production, yes.

[24:29] Markus Klein: Great.

[24:31] Tom Bowers: One more: data residency. For now, US is fine.

[24:35] Markus Klein: Cool.

[24:38] Alex Romero: Since we mentioned competitors—Together, SageMaker endpoints—one thing they struggle with is “explain why performance changed.” Your telemetry seems to help.

[24:48] Omar El-Sayed: That’s the goal. The “selection trace” is basically “why.”

[24:53] Danielle Chen: Are dashboards included or extra?

[24:57] Markus Klein: In Dedicated, you get baseline dashboards. For deeper retention it can be an add-on, but for POC we’ll make sure you have what you need.

[25:06] Priya Nair: Ok.

[25:08] (crosstalk)

[25:09] Danielle Chen: Sorry, one more technical: does selection happen before compilation or after? Like, do you compile per kernel?

[25:17] Yuki Sato: Selection happens at runtime before execution, but after we know the model config. Some kernels have compiled variants. We keep a compilation cache keyed on model+kernel+sku and reuse.

[25:32] Alex Romero: So if kernel changes, you might hit compile overhead?

[25:36] Yuki Sato: Potentially on first use, yes. That’s another reason we avoid flapping and we cache decisions.

[25:44] Omar El-Sayed: And in canary we warm it up before measuring.

[25:49] Danielle Chen: Great.

[25:52] Priya Nair: I think we’ve got what we need. Can we recap deliverables?

[25:57] Markus Klein: Sure, let’s do a recap now and then we can use remaining time for any last Qs.

[26:03] Markus Klein: Redwood side: we’ll send benchmark harness instructions, plus recommended prompt/decode distributions for long-context summarization. We’ll provision a Dedicated test pool in us-east with A100-80GB and L40S. We’ll set v2 behind a gate, hints off, and run v1 vs v2 comparisons focusing on prefill p95/p99.

[26:27] Markus Klein: Northwind side: you’ll confirm target GPUs and whether MIG is enabled. You’ll share sanitized length distributions and concurrency targets. And then we schedule a follow-up to review results.

[26:40] Priya Nair: Sounds right.

[26:42] Tom Bowers: And you’ll also share how rollback works, like the admin control and audit.

[26:47] Ethan Park: Yep, I’ll include that in a note.

[26:51] Omar El-Sayed: I’ll send the telemetry field list and recommended sampling for canary.

[26:56] Yuki Sato: And I’ll send the driver guardrail summary for the SKUs you care about.

[27:01] Danielle Chen: Perfect.

[27:04] Alex Romero: While we have you, can I ask something about L40S? We saw weirdness around attention “flash” kernel. Do you use flash attention?

[27:13] Yuki Sato: We have flash-style kernels, yes. But exact implementation differs by SKU and driver. Some combinations are on denylist. So for L40S we might pick a different kernel than on A100.

[27:28] Alex Romero: Cool. That’s what we wanted.

[27:31] Priya Nair: Ok, last question for me: if we later go Private deployment, does the same selector exist?

[27:38] Markus Klein: Yes. Same runtime, same selection logic. Control plane differs but the knobs exist. For air-gapped, we ship the policy config with the package.

[27:50] Tom Bowers: Good.

[27:52] Markus Klein: Anything else?

[27:54] Danielle Chen: Not from me.

[27:56] Alex Romero: No, good.

[27:58] Priya Nair: All good.

[28:00] Markus Klein: Great. We’ll follow up tomorrow with the recap and proposed benchmark schedule. Thanks all.

[28:07] (meeting continues with light scheduling chatter; transcript truncated by Fireflies)

Notes
- During the call, Redwood referenced internal dashboard panels: selection distribution by model/GPU/seq bucket, prefill/decode latency breakdowns, and rollback trigger thresholds.
- Northwind indicated primary workload: long-context summarization (12k-30k input tokens) with moderate decode (300-1200 output tokens) and sensitivity to p99.
- Benchmark to prioritize A100-80GB and L40S; MIG considerations to be confirmed.

source.transcript:
MEETING HEADER (Fireflies)
Title: Northwind Analytics - technical deep dive: tail latency + KV cache behavior
Date: Jan 14, 2026
Time: 10:06 AM - 11:02 AM PT
Duration: 56 minutes
Participants:
- Nikhil Sharma (Redwood Inference)
- Omar El-Sayed (Redwood Inference)
- Mateo Alvarez (Redwood Inference)
- Sean Gallagher (Redwood Inference)
- Allison Grant (Redwood Inference)
- Priya Desai (Northwind Analytics)
- Evan Brooks (Northwind Analytics)
- Jing Wu (Northwind Analytics)
- Tomás Alvarez (Northwind Analytics)

AUTO-GENERATED SUMMARY
Northwind described burst-driven p99 latency spikes in chat-style workloads with mixed prompt lengths, plus occasional GPU OOM / request shedding on their current stack. Redwood walked through how KV cache allocation patterns and fragmentation can create tail latency under churn, especially when long-lived sessions coexist with short requests and cancellation/retry behavior. Redwood outlined planned runtime improvements: fragmentation measurement, budgeted compaction integrated with the scheduler, and tier-aware eviction (LRU-ish) with kill-switches and observability. Discussion covered what signals to capture (decode vs prefill timing, allocator arena health, bytes moved), and how to run a benchmark that reproduces Northwind’s traffic shape.

TOPICS
- What “tail latency” looks like in mixed-length decode workloads
- KV cache basics (pages/blocks), long-lived allocations, churn, cancellations
- Fragmentation: why it happens, what it costs, why it triggers at the wrong time
- Compaction: incremental vs background, budgets, scheduler integration
- Eviction policy: tier-aware + LRU, pinned sessions, prefix caching interactions
- Guardrails: backpressure, kill-switch, rollback criteria, metrics
- Benchmark ask + what Redwood needs from Northwind

QUESTIONS (captured)
- Can Redwood separate prefill latency from decode latency per request? (Yes, and we can share how we instrument it.)
- How does compaction avoid stalling decode? (Budgeted moves tied to scheduler boundaries; can pause under load.)
- Does eviction affect correctness or output stability? (No correctness change; could impact cache hit rates, which affects latency/cost.)
- Can Dedicated/VPC reduce tail by avoiding noisy neighbors? (Often helps, but fragmentation is still a per-process issue; runtime fixes matter either way.)

TRANSCRIPT
[00:00] Nikhil Sharma: Cool. I think we’re all here. Thanks for making time. This is Nikhil on product at Redwood. We wanted to do a deeper technical session on the tail latency you’ve been seeing, and specifically KV cache behavior, because that’s the place we think a lot of the burst p99 comes from. Quick intros?

[00:17] Priya Desai: Yep. Priya, I run platform engineering here. I’m the one who’s been yelling about p99s, so, hi.

[00:23] Evan Brooks: Evan, ML infra. I’m mostly on the model serving side, GPUs, that stuff.

[00:28] Jing Wu: Jing, SRE. I’m here to translate the alerts into human language.

[00:32] Tomás Alvarez: Tomás, security and compliance. I’m mostly listening unless we end up in VPC or key management land.

[00:39] Mateo Alvarez: Hey all, Mateo. I lead serving runtime engineering at Redwood. (Different Alvarez, no relation.)

[00:45] Omar El-Sayed: Omar, KV cache / memory systems on the runtime side.

MEETING HEADER (Fireflies)
Title: Northwind Analytics - technical deep dive: tail latency + KV cache behavior
Date: Jan 14, 2026
Time: 10:06 AM - 11:02 AM PT
Duration: 56 minutes
Participants:
- Nikhil Sharma (Redwood Inference)
- Omar El-Sayed (Redwood Inference)
- Mateo Alvarez (Redwood Inference)
- Sean Gallagher (Redwood Inference)
- Allison Grant (Redwood Inference)
- Priya Desai (Northwind Analytics)
- Evan Brooks (Northwind Analytics)
- Jing Wu (Northwind Analytics)
- Tomás Alvarez (Northwind Analytics)

AUTO-GENERATED SUMMARY
Northwind described burst-driven p99 latency spikes in chat-style workloads with mixed prompt lengths, plus occasional GPU OOM / request shedding on their current stack. Redwood walked through how KV cache allocation patterns and fragmentation can create tail latency under churn, especially when long-lived sessions coexist with short requests and cancellation/retry behavior. Redwood outlined planned runtime improvements: fragmentation measurement, budgeted compaction integrated with the scheduler, and tier-aware eviction (LRU-ish) with kill-switches and observability. Discussion covered what signals to capture (decode vs prefill timing, allocator arena health, bytes moved), and how to run a benchmark that reproduces Northwind’s traffic shape.

TOPICS
- What “tail latency” looks like in mixed-length decode workloads
- KV cache basics (pages/blocks), long-lived allocations, churn, cancellations
- Fragmentation: why it happens, what it costs, why it triggers at the wrong time
- Compaction: incremental vs background, budgets, scheduler integration
- Eviction policy: tier-aware + LRU, pinned sessions, prefix caching interactions
- Guardrails: backpressure, kill-switch, rollback criteria, metrics
- Benchmark ask + what Redwood needs from Northwind

QUESTIONS (captured)
- Can Redwood separate prefill latency from decode latency per request? (Yes, and we can share how we instrument it.)
- How does compaction avoid stalling decode? (Budgeted moves tied to scheduler boundaries; can pause under load.)
- Does eviction affect correctness or output stability? (No correctness change; could impact cache hit rates, which affects latency/cost.)
- Can Dedicated/VPC reduce tail by avoiding noisy neighbors? (Often helps, but fragmentation is still a per-process issue; runtime fixes matter either way.)

TRANSCRIPT

We wanted to do a deeper technical session on the tail latency you’ve been seeing, and specifically KV cache behavior, because that’s the place we think a lot of the burst p99 comes from. Quick intros?

[00:17] Priya Desai: Yep. Priya, I run platform engineering here. I’m the one who’s been yelling about p99s, so, hi.

[00:23] Evan Brooks: Evan, ML infra. I’m mostly on the model serving side, GPUs, that stuff.

[00:28] Jing Wu: Jing, SRE. I’m here to translate the alerts into human language.

[00:32] Tomás Alvarez: Tomás, security and compliance. I’m mostly listening unless we end up in VPC or key management land.

[00:39] Mateo Alvarez: Hey all, Mateo. I lead serving runtime engineering at Redwood. (Different Alvarez, no relation.)

[00:45] Omar El-Sayed: Omar, KV cache / memory systems on the runtime side.

[00:49] Sean Gallagher: Sean, SRE at Redwood. I’m the one who gets paged when tail latency goes sideways.

[00:54] Allison Grant: Allison, observability standards. Metrics, dashboards, alerts.

[01:00] Nikhil Sharma: Great. Priya, do you want to set context? Like what you’re seeing, and what “bad” looks like for you.

[01:08] Priya Desai: Sure. So we have a chat product. It’s mostly internal analysts, but at peak we’ll get, like, a bunch of people running similar workflows at once. It’s bursty. Our p50 is fine. Even p95 is fine. Then p99 goes to, like, 6–12 seconds. Sometimes more. And it’s not every hour, it’s like, you know, 10 minutes of pain.

[01:31] Priya Desai: We also sometimes see the server process OOM and then we shed requests. That’s worst case. We’ve tried lowering max tokens and stuff, but it doesn’t fully solve it.

[01:44] Jing Wu: The graphs look like sawtooth. Latency is calm, then spikes, then calm.
