[02:04] Nikhil Sharma: Got it. When you say OOM, is it GPU memory, CPU, both?

[02:09] Evan Brooks: GPU. We’ll see “CUDA out of memory” in logs. Sometimes it recovers, sometimes it spirals.

[02:14] Mateo Alvarez: Okay. This is very consistent with what we see in production inference systems when KV cache is stressed. Let me ask a couple clarifying things, then Omar can go deep.

[02:26] Mateo Alvarez: 1) Are these single-turn requests or multi-turn sessions where you keep history?

[02:31] Priya Desai: Multi-turn. We keep some context. But we cap it. Still, sessions can last a while.

[02:38] Mateo Alvarez: 2) Do you do cancellations? Like user hits stop, or client times out and retries?

[02:45] Jing Wu: Yes. We have timeouts. If it hits, we cancel. The client also sometimes retries if it doesn’t get a first token quickly.

[02:53] Evan Brooks: And users spam refresh. It’s a thing.

[02:56] Mateo Alvarez: Yep. That’s important.

[02:59] Nikhil Sharma: And for success criteria, what do you need? Like p99 under 2 seconds? Or is it more nuanced.

[03:07] Priya Desai: For streaming, first token is the big one. But overall, we want p99 under 3 seconds for the first chunk, and we want fewer of these 10-second outliers.

[03:20] Sean Gallagher: Do you measure TTFT separately today?

[03:23] Jing Wu: We have some metrics but it’s messy. We can usually infer first token from client logs.

[03:28] Allison Grant: That’s fine. We can work with that. Ideally we’ll break it into prefill time, scheduling/queue, then decode cadence.

[03:38] Nikhil Sharma: Cool. Omar, do you want to give the “KV cache and why it causes tail latency” overview?

[03:44] Omar El-Sayed: Yeah. I’ll try to keep it high level but stop me. So KV cache is the per-request memory that stores attention keys and values for each layer. It grows with sequence length and number of layers, heads, etc. Practically, it’s the thing that makes decoding fast because you don’t recompute attention over the whole prefix every token.

[04:09] Omar El-Sayed: The problem is, it’s big and it’s long-lived. In streaming decode, that KV lives for the whole request/session. In multi-turn, depending on implementation, it can live across turns or be partially reused (prefix caching).

[04:25] Evan Brooks: Right. We do some prefix caching, not sure how good it is.

[04:29] Omar El-Sayed: Exactly. Now, most runtimes allocate KV in chunks—pages or blocks—because you don’t want one giant malloc for every request. You keep an arena and give out pages. Under steady state with uniform lengths, it’s okay.

[04:46] Omar El-Sayed: Under mixed lengths + churn, you get fragmentation. Think: long requests keep their pages pinned. Short requests come and go, leaving holes. Over time, free pages are scattered. Then when you get a burst, the allocator may have “enough free memory” in total, but not enough contiguous blocks in the right shape.

[05:10] Priya Desai: That maps to what we see where GPU memory looks like 70% used, but allocation fails.

[05:15] Omar El-Sayed: Exactly. There are two costs: (1) allocation failures or expensive search/defrag logic, and (2) opportunistic evictions at bad times. Some systems will evict cached prefixes or even whole sequences to make room, but that can happen right in the middle of a burst.

[05:34] Jing Wu: When you say evict sequences, do you mean killing requests?

[05:38] Omar El-Sayed: It depends. “Eviction” can mean dropping reusable cached prefixes (so future requests lose cache hit rate), or, if you’re out of memory, you may have to shed requests. In Redwood we separate “cache eviction” from “request preemption,” but the symptom can look similar when you’re at the edge.

[05:57] Sean Gallagher: And from an SRE perspective it looks like: p99 spikes, then OOM, then retries, which makes it worse.

[06:05] Mateo Alvarez: The key is: fragmentation makes the system unpredictable. Even if average utilization is fine, the tail gets hammered.

[06:13] Evan Brooks: Okay. Why does it show up as latency spikes rather than just lower throughput?

[06:18] Omar El-Sayed: Great question. Because the allocator work tends to happen on the critical path at request boundary or at KV growth points. If the runtime has to scan free lists, do remapping, or copy pages, that can steal time from decode scheduling. Even tiny stalls can blow up p99 when the system is already close to saturation.

[06:40] Omar El-Sayed: Another piece: cancellations. If a request is canceled while KV is mid-growth, you can end up with partially freed pages, plus bookkeeping overhead. If that’s frequent, fragmentation is worse.

[06:53] Priya Desai: Yep, we have a lot of cancels.

[06:55] Nikhil Sharma: So, the reason we wanted this call: we have a runtime project underway specifically on KV compaction and a safer eviction policy, to address exactly this burst tail issue.

[07:07] Mateo Alvarez: Let’s describe what we’re building in plain terms.

[07:10] Mateo Alvarez: 1) Measure fragmentation continuously. Not just “used memory,” but “how scattered are the free pages,” a fragmentation ratio, plus how often we fail to allocate a contiguous run.

[07:22] Allison Grant: And we standardize the metrics names so SRE and customers can interpret them consistently.

[07:27] Mateo Alvarez: 2) Add compaction. Meaning: move KV pages around to make free space contiguous again.

[07:33] Evan Brooks: On GPU you’re copying memory around? That sounds expensive.

[07:37] Omar El-Sayed: It can be. That’s why we’re not doing “stop the world and defrag.” We’re doing incremental, budgeted compaction.

[07:46] Omar El-Sayed: The idea is: each scheduler cycle, we allow ourselves to move up to X bytes or spend up to Y microseconds copying KV pages, and we do it at boundaries that minimize impact.

[07:59] Sofia Ivanova (speaker label error): Like at the prefill/decode boundary.

[08:02] Mateo Alvarez: That was… not Sofia on the call, but yes, that’s the concept. (laughs)

[08:06] Omar El-Sayed: Exactly. We integrate with the scheduler so we don’t steal time from decode when we’re under load. If the system is hot, we back off.

[08:18] Jing Wu: How do you choose what to move? Like which pages.

[08:22] Omar El-Sayed: Usually we pick “small holes that block large allocations” and relocate movable segments. There are invariants: we can only move KV that is not currently being read/written by an active kernel, or we do it with synchronization points.

[08:38] Evan Brooks: So does that mean per-token you might be moving stuff?

[08:41] Omar El-Sayed: Not per token. More like, between scheduling phases. And it’s budgeted.

[08:47] Sean Gallagher: Also there’s a kill switch. If we see compaction correlating with latency spikes, we can disable it quickly.

[08:55] Tomás Alvarez: When you say kill switch, how fast? Like config propagation.

[08:59] Nikhil Sharma: Good question. In Hosted we can flip it within minutes; for Dedicated we can also flip, depends on the control plane propagation. We can talk about your requirements later.

[09:11] Mateo Alvarez: Then 3) Eviction policy. If you still hit memory pressure, you want a predictable, tier-aware eviction policy. We don’t want random “drop whatever.”

[09:22] Priya Desai: Tier-aware meaning what, like different customers?

[09:25] Nikhil Sharma: In our platform, yes. Also could map to priority classes in Dedicated. The idea is: if you must evict cache, you evict lowest priority, least recently used sequences or cached prefixes first.

[09:39] Omar El-Sayed: And importantly, we treat pinned sessions differently. If something is pinned (e.g., system prompt cache you really want), we can avoid evicting it unless you are in emergency mode.

[09:52] Evan Brooks: What’s the downside? Like what could go wrong.

[09:55] Mateo Alvarez: The tradeoff is copy overhead and complexity. Compaction moves bytes. If your workload is super sensitive, even small copying can hurt. That’s why we enforce budgets and expose metrics.

[10:10] Allison Grant: We also correlate: compaction bytes moved vs p99. If they rise together, you know you’re compaction-bound.

[10:18] Jing Wu: Can you show that in traces?

[10:20] Allison Grant: Yes. We add tracing spans around compaction steps and eviction decisions, so you can see it as an event on a timeline with a request.

[10:29] Priya Desai: The other concern: correctness. Moving KV around seems scary.

[10:34] Omar El-Sayed: Totally fair. Correctness is the first thing. The move is effectively a copy and pointer update. We have invariants and tests: long-lived sessions, concurrent cancels, partial streaming, etc.

[10:48] Omar El-Sayed: We had an early edge case when a request canceled during relocation—caused page leaks and a stall. We fixed that. It’s exactly why we’re doing staged rollout.

[11:02] Sean Gallagher: Yeah, and we tie rollout to SLO guardrails. If p99 goes above threshold for N minutes, we roll back.

[11:09] Jing Wu: That’s interesting. We’ve had bad experiences with “optimizations” that regress tail.

[11:14] Nikhil Sharma: Same. So we’re trying to be boring operationally.

[11:18] Evan Brooks: Can we go one level deeper on the fragmentation itself? Like, what’s the signal that it’s fragmentation and not, say, queueing or kernel variance.

[11:27] Omar El-Sayed: Yep. So typical signals:
- GPU memory utilization is moderate, but allocation failures happen.
- You see increased time in allocator on request admission.
- You see a rise in “pages requested per token” or “KV allocation retries.”
- Tail spikes correlate with burst churn and cancellation.

[11:49] Omar El-Sayed: Queueing is different: you’ll see request queue depth grow, but allocator time stays flat. Kernel variance is different: you’ll see per-token decode time go up across the board, not just p99.

[12:05] Sean Gallagher: Also, with fragmentation, you can get these weird patterns where TTFT is fine but then mid-stream you see stalls when the sequence needs to grow KV and it can’t find blocks. That shows up as chunk jitter.

[12:17] Priya Desai: We see mid-stream stalls sometimes, yeah.

[12:20] Evan Brooks: How does mixed length cause this more?

[12:23] Omar El-Sayed: Long sequences keep many pages pinned for long periods. Short sequences allocate a few pages, then free them quickly, leaving holes of that small size distributed. Then a new long sequence comes in and needs a bunch of pages contiguous or at least in a pattern that’s efficient for the runtime. The holes don’t help.

[12:45] Evan Brooks: Okay.

[12:47] Nikhil Sharma: I want to make sure we’re being concrete. Your ask from us is “help us reduce p99.” Our ask from you is “help us reproduce your traffic shape.” We can run a benchmark but we need inputs.

[13:00] Priya Desai: What inputs do you need? Because we can’t send raw prompts.

[13:05] Mateo Alvarez: Totally. We don’t need content. We need distributions. Like: prompt length histogram, output length targets, concurrency, inter-arrival patterns (bursts), cancellation rate.

[13:18] Allison Grant: Also whether tool calling / function calling is used. It changes the cadence because you may have early stops.

[13:24] Evan Brooks: We don’t do tool calling yet.

[13:26] Nikhil Sharma: Also: regions. Are you mostly us-east? or multi-region.

[13:31] Jing Wu: Mostly us-east-1 today.

[13:34] Tomás Alvarez: And we need VPC. At least for production. For POC we might do something else, but likely VPC.

[13:41] Nikhil Sharma: Got it. Redwood Private or Dedicated in VPC is an option. Hosted is easiest to start for a benchmark, but we can plan for VPC.

[13:52] Evan Brooks: We currently use a mix: we tried Bedrock, we tried direct OpenAI, and now we self-host some open weights using vLLM. Tail is worse on self-host when bursts happen, but cost is better.

[14:06] Nikhil Sharma: Makes sense.

[14:08] Mateo Alvarez: Just to clarify: Dedicated vs Hosted can change noisy neighbor effects and capacity guarantees, but fragmentation is local to the serving process. So runtime improvements help in both.

[14:21] Priya Desai: Right.

[14:23] Jing Wu: When you do compaction, does it increase cost? Like more GPU time.

[14:27] Omar El-Sayed: There is overhead, yes. But the goal is net improvement because you avoid worst-case stalls and avoid OOM/retries. Usually the tail cost is higher than the steady overhead.

[14:39] Sean Gallagher: And you can configure budgets differently by tier. For example, you might accept more compaction in Dedicated where you have stable workloads, less in ultra-latency sensitive.

[14:49] Priya Desai: You said “configure.” Is that customer exposed?

[14:52] Nikhil Sharma: Today it’s mostly config flags and our internal rollout tooling. We don’t have a polished UI yet. But for enterprise customers we do set policies per deployment.

[15:04] Tomás Alvarez: Are those configs auditable? Like change logs.

[15:07] Nikhil Sharma: Yes. We have audit logs in the control plane, and we can provide change history for the deployment.

[15:14] Allison Grant: Also we tend to define safe defaults and then only tune if needed.

[15:19] Evan Brooks: Can you explain “tier-aware eviction” a bit more. Like, what gets evicted.

[15:24] Omar El-Sayed: Sure. There are multiple caches:
1) The live KV for in-flight requests. You generally can’t evict that without preempting the request.
2) Prefix caches (cached prompts) and reusable KV segments.

[15:39] Omar El-Sayed: Eviction policy mostly targets (2). When memory pressure rises, we evict least recently used cached prefixes first, and we can bias based on priority class. So a low-priority cohort loses cache benefits before a high-priority cohort.

[15:56] Priya Desai: That sounds okay.

[15:58] Evan Brooks: But we’re also worried about the live requests failing allocations.

[16:02] Omar El-Sayed: Right. That’s why compaction exists—to make allocations succeed without needing to kill requests. Eviction helps for future allocations and to avoid OOM. If we still can’t allocate KV for an in-flight request, we need backpressure: stop admitting new work or shed.

[16:20] Sean Gallagher: The “safe behavior” is: when fragmentation ratio is high and allocation retries are increasing, we engage backpressure earlier rather than letting OOM happen.

[16:31] Jing Wu: What does backpressure look like? 429s?

[16:34] Nikhil Sharma: In Hosted, typically yes, you’d see rate limiting or queueing policies. In Dedicated, you can choose queueing vs hard reject.

[16:45] Priya Desai: We would prefer queueing up to some limit, then fail.

[16:48] Sean Gallagher: That’s consistent with how we set it.

[16:51] Evan Brooks: Can we talk about what you’ve seen in benchmarks? Like, how much p99 improvement.

[16:56] Mateo Alvarez: We’re cautious about quoting numbers without matching your workload. But the pattern we’ve seen is: under mixed-length burst scenarios, fragmentation-driven spikes reduce significantly. Think: fewer 10-second outliers, more stable p99.

[17:13] Omar El-Sayed: Also fewer OOM events.

[17:15] Priya Desai: That alone would help.

[17:18] Nikhil Sharma: The right way is: we run a test that recreates your churn. We’ll show before/after with the same model, same GPU class.

[17:32] Mateo Alvarez: We support A100, H100, and some L40S classes depending on deployment. For Dedicated we can match your needs.

[17:41] Evan Brooks: Okay.

[17:57] Jing Wu: Yes please. Our current stack is kind of a black box.

[18:01] Priya Desai: Can you define “fragmentation ratio” in a way we can reason about?

[18:06] Omar El-Sayed: Roughly: (largest contiguous free region / total free). If you have lots of free memory but it’s in tiny chunks, ratio is low. If you have a big contiguous area, ratio is higher.

[18:21] Omar El-Sayed: There are variants because our allocator is page-based, but that’s the intuition.

[18:27] Evan Brooks: Okay. So low ratio means bad.

[18:30] Omar El-Sayed: Exactly.

[18:40] Nikhil Sharma: For benchmark we can do synthetic data, so nothing sensitive. For production, in Dedicated and Private we can support customer-managed networking and stricter retention. We can follow up with our security packet.

[18:55] Tomás Alvarez: Okay, as long as we can keep it synthetic for now.

[18:58] Priya Desai: Yeah, we can generate synthetic lengths.

[19:17] Jing Wu: We can probably do sanitized.

[19:19] Nikhil Sharma: Great.

[19:21] Evan Brooks: Going back to compaction, when does it trigger? Always on? or threshold-based.

[19:27] Omar El-Sayed: Threshold-based. We define a fragmentation trigger plus a memory pressure trigger. If fragmentation is high but we’re not under pressure, we can do gentle background compaction. If we’re under pressure, we do incremental compaction with tighter budgets.

[19:46] Evan Brooks: And if we’re under extreme pressure?

[19:49] Omar El-Sayed: Then we stop admitting new requests, evict caches, and if still failing, we may shed low-priority work.

[21:49] Omar El-Sayed: Yeah, we can run it in our benchmark lab. But we’ll tune it to match your distribution.

- prompt length histogram (p50/p90/p99 tokens)

[22:43] Tomás Alvarez: Okay.

[22:45] Evan Brooks: One more competitor question. We also looked at TensorRT-LLM, because NVIDIA claims low latency. Would this fragmentation problem exist there too.

[22:54] Mateo Alvarez: Any system with KV memory management can hit fragmentation, unless it has a robust allocator and compaction/eviction strategy. Some systems rely on simplistic arenas, which works until mixed workloads.

[23:07] Omar El-Sayed: TensorRT has different memory management strategies, but you can still fragment depending on how you allocate. The “physics” is the same.

[25:40] Jing Wu: Can we talk about what we should monitor during a POC? Like, what’s the “this is going wrong” signal.

[25:47] Sean Gallagher: Sure. For tail latency specifically:
- p99 TTFT and p99 end-to-end
- queue depth / admitted vs rejected
- GPU memory utilization + allocation failure counters
- fragmentation ratio
- compaction step time (max) and bytes moved
- eviction rate (prefix cache) and any request shedding

[26:10] Sean Gallagher: And we set guardrails: if p99 breaches for N minutes AND compaction bytes moved spikes, we disable compaction first. If it continues, we roll back the runtime cohort.

[26:23] Priya Desai: That’s very concrete.

[26:25] Allison Grant: Also we’d like to see correlation: “fragmentation ratio low” then “compaction engaged” then “ratio recovers” then “p99 stabilizes.” If you don’t see that, something is off.

[26:39] Jing Wu: Okay.

[26:41] Evan Brooks: I have a more technical question. When you move KV pages, do you invalidate pointers in kernels? Like how do you handle in-flight compute.

[26:50] Omar El-Sayed: We only move pages at points where we can guarantee there’s no kernel reading that page. In practice: you have a scheduler step that launches decode kernels, then you wait for completion or synchronize at a safe boundary, then you can relocate and update the metadata.

[27:07] Omar El-Sayed: For truly asynchronous pipelines, you need careful coordination, but we’ve already got a notion of “KV segments” with ownership. We move only segments not currently in use.

[27:18] Evan Brooks: So you may add syncs.

[27:20] Omar El-Sayed: Potentially small ones, yes, but we minimize it. That’s why budgets matter.

[27:26] Mateo Alvarez: And note, we aren’t trying to do compaction at sub-millisecond granularity. We do it opportunistically.

[27:34] Evan Brooks: Okay.

[27:36] Priya Desai: What’s the biggest unknown for you with our workload.

[27:40] Mateo Alvarez: Cancellation rate and long-tail prompt lengths. Those are usually the killers.

[27:46] Sean Gallagher: Also your burst pattern. Some workloads ramp, some are a step function. Step functions are harder.

[27:54] Jing Wu: Ours is step function.

[27:56] Sean Gallagher: (laughs) Great.

[27:58] Nikhil Sharma: That helps.

[28:00] Priya Desai: Another thing: we saw p99 get worse after we turned on more aggressive batching. Because it queued more.

[28:08] Mateo Alvarez: Right. Batching improves throughput but can increase queueing latency. That’s separate from fragmentation, but they can interact. If queueing increases, cancellations increase, which increases churn, which increases fragmentation.

[28:24] Omar El-Sayed: Exactly. It’s a feedback loop.

[28:27] Evan Brooks: That’s exactly what we saw. Timeout increases, cancels increase, tail gets worse.

[28:33] Sean Gallagher: So in our config we’d likely tune batching + admission control + compaction together. Not just one knob.

[28:41] Priya Desai: Makes sense.

[28:43] Nikhil Sharma: Okay, let’s land on next steps. We’ll send you a benchmark intake doc. It’s basically a checklist. You send us distributions. We’ll run our mixed-length burst scenario with and without the compaction/eviction improvements enabled.

[28:59] Nikhil Sharma: Then we’ll review results, and decide if it makes sense to move to a Dedicated/VPC POC.

[29:07] Priya Desai: That works.

[29:09] Evan Brooks: Can you also share what settings you think are safe defaults? Like compaction budget.

[29:14] Omar El-Sayed: We can share recommended ranges, but we’ll probably tailor it after we see your model and GPU. But yes, we can share initial defaults for Hosted vs Dedicated.

[29:26] Allison Grant: And we’ll send metric definitions so your team can map them to your dashboards.

[29:31] Jing Wu: Great.

[29:33] Tomás Alvarez: And security packet for VPC, KMS, SOC2, etc.

[29:37] Nikhil Sharma: Yep, I’ll loop in our security team separately. For now we’ll keep content synthetic.

[29:43] Priya Desai: One last question. If compaction is behind a flag, can we turn it on/off in a POC ourselves.

[29:51] Sean Gallagher: In Dedicated/Private, yes, with appropriate access controls. In Hosted, we manage it but we can coordinate changes quickly.

[29:59] Priya Desai: Okay.

[30:01] Evan Brooks: Thanks all. This was helpful.

[30:04] Mateo Alvarez: Thanks. And please, send us those traces if you can. Even just timing breakdowns during a spike.

[30:11] Jing Wu: We’ll try. Might take a day.

[30:13] Nikhil Sharma: Sounds good. We’ll send the checklist today or tomorrow morning. Appreciate it.

[30:20] (call continues with scheduling logistics and email addresses; minor crosstalk)

[31:02] Nikhil Sharma: Alright. We’ll stop the recording here. Thanks everyone.
text:
MEETING HEADER (Fireflies)
Title: Northwind Analytics - technical deep dive: tail latency + KV cache behavior
Date: Jan 14, 2026
Time: 10:06 AM - 11:02 AM PT
Duration: 56 minutes
Participants:
- Nikhil Sharma (Redwood Inference)
- Omar El-Sayed (Redwood Inference)
- Mateo Alvarez (Redwood Inference)
- Sean Gallagher (Redwood Inference)
- Allison Grant (Redwood Inference)
- Priya Desai (Northwind Analytics)
- Evan Brooks (Northwind Analytics)
- Jing Wu (Northwind Analytics)
- Tomás Alvarez (Northwind Analytics)

AUTO-GENERATED SUMMARY
Northwind described burst-driven p99 latency spikes in chat-style workloads with mixed prompt lengths, plus occasional GPU OOM / request shedding on their current stack. Redwood walked through how KV cache allocation patterns and fragmentation can create tail latency under churn, especially when long-lived sessions coexist with short requests and cancellation/retry behavior. Redwood outlined planned runtime improvements: fragmentation measurement, budgeted compaction integrated with the scheduler, and tier-aware eviction (LRU-ish) with kill-switches and observability. Discussion covered what signals to capture (decode vs prefill timing, allocator arena health, bytes moved), and how to run a benchmark that reproduces Northwind’s traffic shape.

TOPICS
- What “tail latency” looks like in mixed-length decode workloads
- KV cache basics (pages/blocks), long-lived allocations, churn, cancellations
- Fragmentation: why it happens, what it costs, why it triggers at the wrong time
- Compaction: incremental vs background, budgets, scheduler integration
- Eviction policy: tier-aware + LRU, pinned sessions, prefix caching interactions
- Guardrails: backpressure, kill-switch, rollback criteria, metrics
- Benchmark ask + what Redwood needs from Northwind

QUESTIONS (captured)
- Can Redwood separate prefill latency from decode latency per request? (Yes, and we can share how we instrument it.)
- How does compaction avoid stalling decode? (Budgeted moves tied to scheduler boundaries; can pause under load.)
- Does eviction affect correctness or output stability? (No correctness change; could impact cache hit rates, which affects latency/cost.)
- Can Dedicated/VPC reduce tail by avoiding noisy neighbors? (Often helps, but fragmentation is still a per-process issue; runtime fixes matter either way.)

TRANSCRIPT
[00:00] Nikhil Sharma: Cool. I think we’re all here. Thanks for making time. This is Nikhil on product at Redwood. We wanted to do a deeper technical session on the tail latency you’ve been seeing, and specifically KV cache behavior, because that’s the place we think a lot of the burst p99 comes from. Quick intros?

[00:17] Priya Desai: Yep. Priya, I run platform engineering here. I’m the one who’s been yelling about p99s, so, hi.

[00:23] Evan Brooks: Evan, ML infra. I’m mostly on the model serving side, GPUs, that stuff.

[00:28] Jing Wu: Jing, SRE. I’m here to translate the alerts into human language.

[00:32] Tomás Alvarez: Tomás, security and compliance. I’m mostly listening unless we end up in VPC or key management land.

[00:39] Mateo Alvarez: Hey all, Mateo. I lead serving runtime engineering at Redwood. (Different Alvarez, no relation.)

[00:45] Omar El-Sayed: Omar, KV cache / memory systems on the runtime side.

[00:49] Sean Gallagher: Sean, SRE at Redwood. I’m the one who gets paged when tail latency goes sideways.

[00:54] Allison Grant: Allison, observability standards. Metrics, dashboards, alerts.

[01:00] Nikhil Sharma: Great. Priya, do you want to set context? Like what you’re seeing, and what “bad” looks like for you.

[01:08] Priya Desai: Sure. So we have a chat product. It’s mostly internal analysts, but at peak we’ll get, like, a bunch of people running similar workflows at once. It’s bursty. Our p50 is fine. Even p95 is fine. Then p99 goes to, like, 6–12 seconds. Sometimes more. And it’s not every hour, it’s like, you know, 10 minutes of pain.

[01:31] Priya Desai: We also sometimes see the server process OOM and then we shed requests. That’s worst case. We’ve tried lowering max tokens and stuff, but it doesn’t fully solve it.

[01:44] Jing Wu: The graphs look like sawtooth. Latency is calm, then spikes, then calm.

[01:49] Evan Brooks: And we run mixed sequence lengths. Some prompts are short, like 200 tokens. Some are giant because people paste tables or logs. And we stream outputs. So decode is long-lived.

[02:04] Nikhil Sharma: Got it. When you say OOM, is it GPU memory, CPU, both?

[02:09] Evan Brooks: GPU. We’ll see “CUDA out of memory” in logs. Sometimes it recovers, sometimes it spirals.

[02:14] Mateo Alvarez: Okay. This is very consistent with what we see in production inference systems when KV cache is stressed. Let me ask a couple clarifying things, then Omar can go deep.

[02:26] Mateo Alvarez: 1) Are these single-turn requests or multi-turn sessions where you keep history?

[02:31] Priya Desai: Multi-turn. We keep some context. But we cap it. Still, sessions can last a while.

[02:38] Mateo Alvarez: 2) Do you do cancellations? Like user hits stop, or client times out and retries?

[02:45] Jing Wu: Yes. We have timeouts. If it hits, we cancel. The client also sometimes retries if it doesn’t get a first token quickly.

[02:53] Evan Brooks: And users spam refresh. It’s a thing.

[02:56] Mateo Alvarez: Yep. That’s important.

[02:59] Nikhil Sharma: And for success criteria, what do you need? Like p99 under 2 seconds? Or is it more nuanced.

[03:07] Priya Desai: For streaming, first token is the big one. But overall, we want p99 under 3 seconds for the first chunk, and we want fewer of these 10-second outliers.

[03:20] Sean Gallagher: Do you measure TTFT separately today?

[03:23] Jing Wu: We have some metrics but it’s messy. We can usually infer first token from client logs.

[03:28] Allison Grant: That’s fine. We can work with that. Ideally we’ll break it into prefill time, scheduling/queue, then decode cadence.

[03:38] Nikhil Sharma: Cool. Omar, do you want to give the “KV cache and why it causes tail latency” overview?

[03:44] Omar El-Sayed: Yeah. I’ll try to keep it high level but stop me. So KV cache is the per-request memory that stores attention keys and values for each layer. It grows with sequence length and number of layers, heads, etc. Practically, it’s the thing that makes decoding fast because you don’t recompute attention over the whole prefix every token.

[04:09] Omar El-Sayed: The problem is, it’s big and it’s long-lived. In streaming decode, that KV lives for the whole request/session. In multi-turn, depending on implementation, it can live across turns or be partially reused (prefix caching).

[04:25] Evan Brooks: Right. We do some prefix caching, not sure how good it is.

[04:29] Omar El-Sayed: Exactly. Now, most runtimes allocate KV in chunks—pages or blocks—because you don’t want one giant malloc for every request. You keep an arena and give out pages. Under steady state with uniform lengths, it’s okay.

[04:46] Omar El-Sayed: Under mixed lengths + churn, you get fragmentation. Think: long requests keep their pages pinned. Short requests come and go, leaving holes. Over time, free pages are scattered. Then when you get a burst, the allocator may have “enough free memory” in total, but not enough contiguous blocks in the right shape.

[05:10] Priya Desai: That maps to what we see where GPU memory looks like 70% used, but allocation fails.

[05:15] Omar El-Sayed: Exactly. There are two costs: (1) allocation failures or expensive search/defrag logic, and (2) opportunistic evictions at bad times. Some systems will evict cached prefixes or even whole sequences to make room, but that can happen right in the middle of a burst.

[05:34] Jing Wu: When you say evict sequences, do you mean killing requests?

[05:38] Omar El-Sayed: It depends. “Eviction” can mean dropping reusable cached prefixes (so future requests lose cache hit rate), or, if you’re out of memory, you may have to shed requests. In Redwood we separate “cache eviction” from “request preemption,” but the symptom can look similar when you’re at the edge.

[05:57] Sean Gallagher: And from an SRE perspective it looks like: p99 spikes, then OOM, then retries, which makes it worse.

[06:05] Mateo Alvarez: The key is: fragmentation makes the system unpredictable. Even if average utilization is fine, the tail gets hammered.

[06:13] Evan Brooks: Okay. Why does it show up as latency spikes rather than just lower throughput?

[06:18] Omar El-Sayed: Great question. Because the allocator work tends to happen on the critical path at request boundary or at KV growth points. If the runtime has to scan free lists, do remapping, or copy pages, that can steal time from decode scheduling. Even tiny stalls can blow up p99 when the system is already close to saturation.

[06:40] Omar El-Sayed: Another piece: cancellations. If a request is canceled while KV is mid-growth, you can end up with partially freed pages, plus bookkeeping overhead. If that’s frequent, fragmentation is worse.

[06:53] Priya Desai: Yep, we have a lot of cancels.

[06:55] Nikhil Sharma: So, the reason we wanted this call: we have a runtime project underway specifically on KV compaction and a safer eviction policy, to address exactly this burst tail issue.

[07:07] Mateo Alvarez: Let’s describe what we’re building in plain terms.

[07:10] Mateo Alvarez: 1) Measure fragmentation continuously. Not just “used memory,” but “how scattered are the free pages,” a fragmentation ratio, plus how often we fail to allocate a contiguous run.

[07:22] Allison Grant: And we standardize the metrics names so SRE and customers can interpret them consistently.

[07:27] Mateo Alvarez: 2) Add compaction. Meaning: move KV pages around to make free space contiguous again.

[07:33] Evan Brooks: On GPU you’re copying memory around? That sounds expensive.

[07:37] Omar El-Sayed: It can be. That’s why we’re not doing “stop the world and defrag.” We’re doing incremental, budgeted compaction.

[07:46] Omar El-Sayed: The idea is: each scheduler cycle, we allow ourselves to move up to X bytes or spend up to Y microseconds copying KV pages, and we do it at boundaries that minimize impact.

[07:59] Sofia Ivanova (speaker label error): Like at the prefill/decode boundary.

[08:02] Mateo Alvarez: That was… not Sofia on the call, but yes, that’s the concept. (laughs)

[08:06] Omar El-Sayed: Exactly. We integrate with the scheduler so we don’t steal time from decode when we’re under load. If the system is hot, we back off.

[08:18] Jing Wu: How do you choose what to move? Like which pages.

[08:22] Omar El-Sayed: Usually we pick “small holes that block large allocations” and relocate movable segments. There are invariants: we can only move KV that is not currently being read/written by an active kernel, or we do it with synchronization points.

[08:38] Evan Brooks: So does that mean per-token you might be moving stuff?

[08:41] Omar El-Sayed: Not per token. More like, between scheduling phases. And it’s budgeted.

[08:47] Sean Gallagher: Also there’s a kill switch. If we see compaction correlating with latency spikes, we can disable it quickly.

[08:55] Tomás Alvarez: When you say kill switch, how fast? Like config propagation.

[08:59] Nikhil Sharma: Good question. In Hosted we can flip it within minutes; for Dedicated we can also flip, depends on the control plane propagation. We can talk about your requirements later.

[09:11] Mateo Alvarez: Then 3) Eviction policy. If you still hit memory pressure, you want a predictable, tier-aware eviction policy. We don’t want random “drop whatever.”

[09:22] Priya Desai: Tier-aware meaning what, like different customers?

[09:25] Nikhil Sharma: In our platform, yes. Also could map to priority classes in Dedicated. The idea is: if you must evict cache, you evict lowest priority, least recently used sequences or cached prefixes first.

[09:39] Omar El-Sayed: And importantly, we treat pinned sessions differently. If something is pinned (e.g., system prompt cache you really want), we can avoid evicting it unless you are in emergency mode.

[09:52] Evan Brooks: What’s the downside? Like what could go wrong.

[09:55] Mateo Alvarez: The tradeoff is copy overhead and complexity. Compaction moves bytes. If your workload is super sensitive, even small copying can hurt. That’s why we enforce budgets and expose metrics.

[10:10] Allison Grant: We also correlate: compaction bytes moved vs p99. If they rise together, you know you’re compaction-bound.

[10:18] Jing Wu: Can you show that in traces?

[10:20] Allison Grant: Yes. We add tracing spans around compaction steps and eviction decisions, so you can see it as an event on a timeline with a request.

[10:29] Priya Desai: The other concern: correctness. Moving KV around seems scary.

[10:34] Omar El-Sayed: Totally fair. Correctness is the first thing. The move is effectively a copy and pointer update. We have invariants and tests: long-lived sessions, concurrent cancels, partial streaming, etc.

[10:48] Omar El-Sayed: We had an early edge case when a request canceled during relocation—caused page leaks and a stall. We fixed that. It’s exactly why we’re doing staged rollout.

[11:02] Sean Gallagher: Yeah, and we tie rollout to SLO guardrails. If p99 goes above threshold for N minutes, we roll back.

[11:09] Jing Wu: That’s interesting. We’ve had bad experiences with “optimizations” that regress tail.

[11:14] Nikhil Sharma: Same. So we’re trying to be boring operationally.

[11:18] Evan Brooks: Can we go one level deeper on the fragmentation itself? Like, what’s the signal that it’s fragmentation and not, say, queueing or kernel variance.

[11:27] Omar El-Sayed: Yep. So typical signals:
- GPU memory utilization is moderate, but allocation failures happen.
- You see increased time in allocator on request admission.
- You see a rise in “pages requested per token” or “KV allocation retries.”
- Tail spikes correlate with burst churn and cancellation.

[11:49] Omar El-Sayed: Queueing is different: you’ll see request queue depth grow, but allocator time stays flat. Kernel variance is different: you’ll see per-token decode time go up across the board, not just p99.

[12:05] Sean Gallagher: Also, with fragmentation, you can get these weird patterns where TTFT is fine but then mid-stream you see stalls when the sequence needs to grow KV and it can’t find blocks. That shows up as chunk jitter.

[12:17] Priya Desai: We see mid-stream stalls sometimes, yeah.

[12:20] Evan Brooks: How does mixed length cause this more?

[12:23] Omar El-Sayed: Long sequences keep many pages pinned for long periods. Short sequences allocate a few pages, then free them quickly, leaving holes of that small size distributed. Then a new long sequence comes in and needs a bunch of pages contiguous or at least in a pattern that’s efficient for the runtime. The holes don’t help.

[12:45] Evan Brooks: Okay.

[12:47] Nikhil Sharma: I want to make sure we’re being concrete. Your ask from us is “help us reduce p99.” Our ask from you is “help us reproduce your traffic shape.” We can run a benchmark but we need inputs.

[13:00] Priya Desai: What inputs do you need? Because we can’t send raw prompts.

[13:05] Mateo Alvarez: Totally. We don’t need content. We need distributions. Like: prompt length histogram, output length targets, concurrency, inter-arrival patterns (bursts), cancellation rate.

[13:18] Allison Grant: Also whether tool calling / function calling is used. It changes the cadence because you may have early stops.

[13:24] Evan Brooks: We don’t do tool calling yet.

[13:26] Nikhil Sharma: Also: regions. Are you mostly us-east? or multi-region.

[13:31] Jing Wu: Mostly us-east-1 today.

[13:34] Tomás Alvarez: And we need VPC. At least for production. For POC we might do something else, but likely VPC.

[13:41] Nikhil Sharma: Got it. Redwood Private or Dedicated in VPC is an option. Hosted is easiest to start for a benchmark, but we can plan for VPC.

[13:52] Evan Brooks: We currently use a mix: we tried Bedrock, we tried direct OpenAI, and now we self-host some open weights using vLLM. Tail is worse on self-host when bursts happen, but cost is better.

[14:06] Nikhil Sharma: Makes sense.

[14:08] Mateo Alvarez: Just to clarify: Dedicated vs Hosted can change noisy neighbor effects and capacity guarantees, but fragmentation is local to the serving process. So runtime improvements help in both.

[14:21] Priya Desai: Right.

[14:23] Jing Wu: When you do compaction, does it increase cost? Like more GPU time.

[14:27] Omar El-Sayed: There is overhead, yes. But the goal is net improvement because you avoid worst-case stalls and avoid OOM/retries. Usually the tail cost is higher than the steady overhead.

[14:39] Sean Gallagher: And you can configure budgets differently by tier. For example, you might accept more compaction in Dedicated where you have stable workloads, less in ultra-latency sensitive.

[14:49] Priya Desai: You said “configure.” Is that customer exposed?

[14:52] Nikhil Sharma: Today it’s mostly config flags and our internal rollout tooling. We don’t have a polished UI yet. But for enterprise customers we do set policies per deployment.

[15:04] Tomás Alvarez: Are those configs auditable? Like change logs.

[15:07] Nikhil Sharma: Yes. We have audit logs in the control plane, and we can provide change history for the deployment.

[15:14] Allison Grant: Also we tend to define safe defaults and then only tune if needed.

[15:19] Evan Brooks: Can you explain “tier-aware eviction” a bit more. Like, what gets evicted.

[15:24] Omar El-Sayed: Sure. There are multiple caches:
1) The live KV for in-flight requests. You generally can’t evict that without preempting the request.
2) Prefix caches (cached prompts) and reusable KV segments.

[15:39] Omar El-Sayed: Eviction policy mostly targets (2). When memory pressure rises, we evict least recently used cached prefixes first, and we can bias based on priority class. So a low-priority cohort loses cache benefits before a high-priority cohort.

[15:56] Priya Desai: That sounds okay.

[15:58] Evan Brooks: But we’re also worried about the live requests failing allocations.

[16:02] Omar El-Sayed: Right. That’s why compaction exists—to make allocations succeed without needing to kill requests. Eviction helps for future allocations and to avoid OOM. If we still can’t allocate KV for an in-flight request, we need backpressure: stop admitting new work or shed.

[16:20] Sean Gallagher: The “safe behavior” is: when fragmentation ratio is high and allocation retries are increasing, we engage backpressure earlier rather than letting OOM happen.

[16:31] Jing Wu: What does backpressure look like? 429s?

[16:34] Nikhil Sharma: In Hosted, typically yes, you’d see rate limiting or queueing policies. In Dedicated, you can choose queueing vs hard reject.

[16:45] Priya Desai: We would prefer queueing up to some limit, then fail.

[16:48] Sean Gallagher: That’s consistent with how we set it.

[16:51] Evan Brooks: Can we talk about what you’ve seen in benchmarks? Like, how much p99 improvement.

[16:56] Mateo Alvarez: We’re cautious about quoting numbers without matching your workload. But the pattern we’ve seen is: under mixed-length burst scenarios, fragmentation-driven spikes reduce significantly. Think: fewer 10-second outliers, more stable p99.

[17:13] Omar El-Sayed: Also fewer OOM events.

[17:15] Priya Desai: That alone would help.

[17:18] Nikhil Sharma: The right way is: we run a test that recreates your churn. We’ll show before/after with the same model, same GPU class.

[17:28] Evan Brooks: Speaking of GPUs, what do you run? We run A100s right now.

[17:32] Mateo Alvarez: We support A100, H100, and some L40S classes depending on deployment. For Dedicated we can match your needs.

[17:41] Evan Brooks: Okay.

[17:43] Allison Grant: One more thing: metrics. If we do a POC, we want to give you visibility: fragmentation ratio, compaction time, bytes moved, eviction counts, and correlate with p99. That way you’re not blind.

[17:57] Jing Wu: Yes please. Our current stack is kind of a black box.

[18:01] Priya Desai: Can you define “fragmentation ratio” in a way we can reason about?

[18:06] Omar El-Sayed: Roughly: (largest contiguous free region / total free). If you have lots of free memory but it’s in tiny chunks, ratio is low. If you have a big contiguous area, ratio is higher.

[18:21] Omar El-Sayed: There are variants because our allocator is page-based, but that’s the intuition.

[18:27] Evan Brooks: Okay. So low ratio means bad.

[18:30] Omar El-Sayed: Exactly.

[18:32] Tomás Alvarez: Sorry, quick compliance question. If we go Hosted for initial benchmark, what’s the data policy? Prompts/logging retention.

[18:40] Nikhil Sharma: For benchmark we can do synthetic data, so nothing sensitive. For production, in Dedicated and Private we can support customer-managed networking and stricter retention. We can follow up with our security packet.

[18:55] Tomás Alvarez: Okay, as long as we can keep it synthetic for now.

[18:58] Priya Desai: Yeah, we can generate synthetic lengths.

[19:01] Evan Brooks: But we need it to match our structure. Because tokenization differs if you have tables.

[19:07] Mateo Alvarez: Fair point. You can approximate with random text of similar token length. Or we can take a handful of sanitized prompts. Totally up to your policies.

[19:17] Jing Wu: We can probably do sanitized.

[19:19] Nikhil Sharma: Great.

[19:21] Evan Brooks: Going back to compaction, when does it trigger? Always on? or threshold-based.

[19:27] Omar El-Sayed: Threshold-based. We define a fragmentation trigger plus a memory pressure trigger. If fragmentation is high but we’re not under pressure, we can do gentle background compaction. If we’re under pressure, we do incremental compaction with tighter budgets.

[19:46] Evan Brooks: And if we’re under extreme pressure?

[19:49] Omar El-Sayed: Then we stop admitting new requests, evict caches, and if still failing, we may shed low-priority work.

[19:57] Sean Gallagher: And that behavior is configurable; also, we alert on it. You should never be surprised by eviction.

[20:04] Jing Wu: That would be nice. We often only find out when customers complain.

[20:09] Nikhil Sharma: Same.

[20:12] Priya Desai: Can you talk about why you integrate it with scheduler boundaries. Like what does that mean. We run vLLM and it batches.

[20:20] Mateo Alvarez: Right. Most modern runtimes do continuous batching: they interleave prefill and decode across requests. If you do a heavy compaction copy at the wrong time, you can delay the next decode step for everyone in the batch. That shows up as tail.

[20:38] Mateo Alvarez: So we enforce compaction budgets at safe points: for example, at the end of a decode iteration, or when there’s a natural pause, so you don’t cause a multi-millisecond stall.

[20:51] Evan Brooks: So it’s not a separate thread doing whatever.

[20:54] Omar El-Sayed: We may have a background worker, but it’s still coordinated. The key is: compaction can be paused. It’s incremental.

[21:02] Priya Desai: That’s the part we wanted to hear.

[21:06] Allison Grant: And we can report “compaction time per minute” and “max compaction step time” so you can see if steps are too chunky.

[21:16] Jing Wu: Nice.

[21:18] Nikhil Sharma: Let’s pivot to benchmark. Evan, if we asked for three scenarios, what would you want to test?

[21:25] Evan Brooks: One is steady load, like normal day. Two is burst: 5x traffic for 10 minutes. Three is mixed lengths, like heavy tail of long prompts.

[21:35] Priya Desai: And cancellations. We want to simulate the timeouts.

[21:38] Omar El-Sayed: Perfect. That’s basically what we call “mixed-length burst with churn.” We have a workload generator for it.

[21:47] Evan Brooks: Do you?

[21:49] Omar El-Sayed: Yeah, we can run it in our benchmark lab. But we’ll tune it to match your distribution.

[21:57] Priya Desai: What do you need from us to tune it.

[22:01] Allison Grant: If you can give:
- prompt length histogram (p50/p90/p99 tokens)
- output length targets (max/new tokens)
- inter-arrival pattern for bursts
- cancellation probability and timing
- concurrency target

[22:18] Allison Grant: Also streaming on/off, and whether you do server-side batching constraints.

[22:25] Jing Wu: We can do that.

[22:27] Tomás Alvarez: And we’d want to know where metrics live. Can you export to our Datadog?

[22:32] Sean Gallagher: For Dedicated/Private, yes, we can integrate. For Hosted, we provide dashboards and can push certain metrics. We can discuss specifics.

[22:43] Tomás Alvarez: Okay.

[22:45] Evan Brooks: One more competitor question. We also looked at TensorRT-LLM, because NVIDIA claims low latency. Would this fragmentation problem exist there too.

[22:54] Mateo Alvarez: Any system with KV memory management can hit fragmentation, unless it has a robust allocator and compaction/eviction strategy. Some systems rely on simplistic arenas, which works until mixed workloads.

[23:07] Omar El-Sayed: TensorRT has different memory management strategies, but you can still fragment depending on how you allocate. The “physics” is the same.

[23:15] Priya Desai: So what’s unique here is the compaction plus scheduler integration plus observability.

[23:20] Nikhil Sharma: That’s how we think about it, yes.

[23:23] Jing Wu: How soon is this available? Is it GA?

[23:27] Mateo Alvarez: It’s in active rollout internally. We’re targeting runtime 1.22 for broader availability. We can potentially get you into an early cohort depending on timeline.

[23:39] Priya Desai: Our timeline is… we want to decide by end of February.

[23:44] Nikhil Sharma: That aligns.

[23:46] Sean Gallagher: If we do an early cohort, we’ll be strict about guardrails and rollback. We won’t leave you holding the bag.

[23:54] Priya Desai: Good.

[23:56] Evan Brooks: Do we have to change our app code to benefit?

[24:00] Nikhil Sharma: No. It’s runtime behavior. You may want to send some headers or choose a tier/priority for certain traffic, but baseline improvements apply without app changes.

[24:12] Priya Desai: That’s great.

[24:14] Jing Wu: What about function calling? Not now but later.

[24:17] Mateo Alvarez: That’s fine. It changes request patterns but the compaction logic still holds.

[24:23] Evan Brooks: I’d like to understand how you avoid compaction thrash. Like you compact, then new long requests come and fragment again.

[24:31] Omar El-Sayed: Great point. We combine compaction with smarter free-list management. So we try to allocate in a way that keeps like-with-like. Also, compaction is not constant; it only runs when fragmentation exceeds a threshold and it has a budget.

[24:48] Omar El-Sayed: So you don’t get into a “copy forever” loop unless your workload is extremely adversarial. And if it is, you’ll see it in metrics, and we can adjust.

[24:57] Allison Grant: One metric we like is “bytes moved per token generated.” If that climbs, you know compaction is doing too much work.

[25:05] Evan Brooks: Nice.

[25:07] Priya Desai: For us, the pain is these rare spikes. If compaction adds 2% overhead but removes spikes, we’d take it.

[25:15] Mateo Alvarez: That’s exactly the trade we’re targeting.

[25:19] Nikhil Sharma: Tomás, anything else from security? We can keep it short.

[25:23] Tomás Alvarez: Not much, just: if we go VPC, we’ll need encryption at rest and in transit, and ideally customer-managed keys. And audit logs.

[25:31] Nikhil Sharma: Yep. We can handle that in Dedicated/Private. For this call, we’ll keep benchmark synthetic.

[25:40] Jing Wu: Can we talk about what we should monitor during a POC? Like, what’s the “this is going wrong” signal.

[25:47] Sean Gallagher: Sure. For tail latency specifically:
- p99 TTFT and p99 end-to-end
- queue depth / admitted vs rejected
- GPU memory utilization + allocation failure counters
- fragmentation ratio
- compaction step time (max) and bytes moved
- eviction rate (prefix cache) and any request shedding

[26:10] Sean Gallagher: And we set guardrails: if p99 breaches for N minutes AND compaction bytes moved spikes, we disable compaction first. If it continues, we roll back the runtime cohort.

[26:23] Priya Desai: That’s very concrete.

[26:25] Allison Grant: Also we’d like to see correlation: “fragmentation ratio low” then “compaction engaged” then “ratio recovers” then “p99 stabilizes.” If you don’t see that, something is off.

[26:39] Jing Wu: Okay.

[26:41] Evan Brooks: I have a more technical question. When you move KV pages, do you invalidate pointers in kernels? Like how do you handle in-flight compute.

[26:50] Omar El-Sayed: We only move pages at points where we can guarantee there’s no kernel reading that page. In practice: you have a scheduler step that launches decode kernels, then you wait for completion or synchronize at a safe boundary, then you can relocate and update the metadata.

[27:07] Omar El-Sayed: For truly asynchronous pipelines, you need careful coordination, but we’ve already got a notion of “KV segments” with ownership. We move only segments not currently in use.

[27:18] Evan Brooks: So you may add syncs.

[27:20] Omar El-Sayed: Potentially small ones, yes, but we minimize it. That’s why budgets matter.

[27:26] Mateo Alvarez: And note, we aren’t trying to do compaction at sub-millisecond granularity. We do it opportunistically.

[27:34] Evan Brooks: Okay.

[27:36] Priya Desai: What’s the biggest unknown for you with our workload.

[27:40] Mateo Alvarez: Cancellation rate and long-tail prompt lengths. Those are usually the killers.

[27:46] Sean Gallagher: Also your burst pattern. Some workloads ramp, some are a step function. Step functions are harder.

[27:54] Jing Wu: Ours is step function.

[27:56] Sean Gallagher: (laughs) Great.

[27:58] Nikhil Sharma: That helps.

[28:00] Priya Desai: Another thing: we saw p99 get worse after we turned on more aggressive batching. Because it queued more.

[28:08] Mateo Alvarez: Right. Batching improves throughput but can increase queueing latency. That’s separate from fragmentation, but they can interact. If queueing increases, cancellations increase, which increases churn, which increases fragmentation.

[28:24] Omar El-Sayed: Exactly. It’s a feedback loop.

[28:27] Evan Brooks: That’s exactly what we saw. Timeout increases, cancels increase, tail gets worse.

[28:33] Sean Gallagher: So in our config we’d likely tune batching + admission control + compaction together. Not just one knob.

[28:41] Priya Desai: Makes sense.

[28:43] Nikhil Sharma: Okay, let’s land on next steps. We’ll send you a benchmark intake doc. It’s basically a checklist. You send us distributions. We’ll run our mixed-length burst scenario with and without the compaction/eviction improvements enabled.

[28:59] Nikhil Sharma: Then we’ll review results, and decide if it makes sense to move to a Dedicated/VPC POC.

[29:07] Priya Desai: That works.

[29:09] Evan Brooks: Can you also share what settings you think are safe defaults? Like compaction budget.

[29:14] Omar El-Sayed: We can share recommended ranges, but we’ll probably tailor it after we see your model and GPU. But yes, we can share initial defaults for Hosted vs Dedicated.

[29:26] Allison Grant: And we’ll send metric definitions so your team can map them to your dashboards.

[29:31] Jing Wu: Great.

[29:33] Tomás Alvarez: And security packet for VPC, KMS, SOC2, etc.

[29:37] Nikhil Sharma: Yep, I’ll loop in our security team separately. For now we’ll keep content synthetic.

[29:43] Priya Desai: One last question. If compaction is behind a flag, can we turn it on/off in a POC ourselves.

[29:51] Sean Gallagher: In Dedicated/Private, yes, with appropriate access controls. In Hosted, we manage it but we can coordinate changes quickly.

[29:59] Priya Desai: Okay.

[30:01] Evan Brooks: Thanks all. This was helpful.

[30:04] Mateo Alvarez: Thanks. And please, send us those traces if you can. Even just timing breakdowns during a spike.

[30:11] Jing Wu: We’ll try. Might take a day.

[30:13] Nikhil Sharma: Sounds good. We’ll send the checklist today or tomorrow morning. Appreciate it.

[30:20] (call continues with scheduling logistics and email addresses; minor crosstalk)

[31:02] Nikhil Sharma: Alright. We’ll stop the recording here. Thanks everyone.

source.title: Northwind Analytics x Redwood - POC Check-in
next_steps: Redwood to share guidance on keeping JSON Schema constraints small (avoid anyOf/oneOf)
Northwind to test tool-calling fallback for large schemas
Redwood to confirm us-east mitigation rollout timing
source.transcript:
Meeting header
Date: 2025-08-01
Time: 11:00 AM PT
Duration: 38 min
Attendees: Ben Carter (Redwood), Aisha Rahman (Redwood), Samir Qureshi (Northwind), Nina Gomez (Northwind)

Summary
Northwind reports intermittent timeouts when using Redwood hosted API structured output with JSON Schema. Redwood shares mitigations and confirms an in-progress config change in us-east.

Transcript
[00:00] Ben Carter: Hey Samir, Nina. Thanks for jumping on. Hows the POC going overall?
[00:14] Samir Qureshi: Pretty good, except were kind of stuck on the structured output thing. When we enable the JSON schema, well hit timeouts. Like not always, but enough to break the workflow.
[00:33] Nina Gomez: Yeah its the same prompt. If we turn schema off, it returns. With schema on, sometimes it just hangs and then we hit 60 seconds on our side.
[00:49] Aisha Rahman: Got it. That matches what were seeing on our side: long tail latency on the constrained decoding path. Do you remember if its worse with the bigger schema?
[01:03] Samir Qureshi: Yeah, bigger schema is definitely worse. Ours is like, dozens of fields, nested arrays, and we had a few any-of sections.
[01:18] Ben Carter: Okay. Two things to try immediately: reduce max output tokens, and simplify the schema. The anyOf/oneOf can blow up the constraint space.
[01:34] Samir Qureshi: That makes sense. But we need the schema ideally. Do you have a recommended limit?
[01:45] Aisha Rahman: We dont have a hard limit published yet. Internally were adding more guardrails. For now well share best practices: keep it flat, avoid combinators, and if you need optional branches, consider tool calling.
[02:09] Nina Gomez: Tool calling worked but our downstream system expects JSON in a very specific format, thats why the schema thing is nice.
[02:23] Ben Carter: Totally. Also were rolling out a mitigation in us-east for enterprise traffic that extends the internal step timeout. It should reduce the gateway timeouts youre seeing.
[02:38] Samir Qureshi: Is that already live?
[02:42] Aisha Rahman: Not yet, but soon. Well email when its fully deployed.
[03:02] Samir Qureshi: Okay. Just to confirm: this isnt only us, right?
[03:07] Ben Carter: No, weve had at least one other report. Were treating it as a known issue and well publish a doc so your team has something to reference.
[03:23] Nina Gomez: Cool. If you can share that doc plus guidance, we can adjust the schema and keep going.
[03:35] Ben Carter: Sounds good. Lets align on next steps: well send schema best practices + confirm mitigation timing; you test with a simplified schema and possibly tool-calling fallback for the larger ones.
[03:53] Samir Qureshi: Yep.
